---

# ⚡「 Chapter 1.9 」— Map-Reduce Patterns in LangGraph

> <span style="color:#00D4FF">**Advanced LangGraph**</span> · Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐ · Read Time: ~25 min

---

## 🔵 1.9.1 「 Also Known As 」

| Alias | Context |
|-------|---------|
| **Scatter-Gather** | Enterprise integration patterns terminology |
| **Fan-Out / Fan-In** | LangGraph documentation and distributed systems |
| **Parallel Map + Merge** | Functional programming tradition |
| **Fork-Join** | Java/concurrent programming terminology |
| **Distribute-Collect** | Data pipeline terminology |
| **Parallel Aggregation** | When the reduce step aggregates results |
| **Batch Parallel Processing** | When mapping over a batch of items |
| **Split-Process-Combine** | Descriptive three-phase name |

**One-line definition:** Map-reduce in LangGraph is the pattern of dynamically fanning out work across multiple parallel branches (map), then collecting and merging results into a single state (reduce), using either static parallel edges or LangGraph's `Send()` API for dynamic parallelism.

---

## 🔵 1.9.2 「 The Layman Explanation 」

Imagine you're a **restaurant manager** doing inventory. You need counts from **five storage areas**: walk-in fridge, freezer, dry storage, bar, and prep station. You have two options:

**Option A (Sequential):** Walk to each area yourself, count items, walk back, repeat. Takes 5× the time.

**Option B (Map-Reduce):** Hand a clipboard to five staff members simultaneously. Each counts their area (the **map** phase). They all bring their clipboards back, and you combine the totals into one master inventory (the **reduce** phase).

In LangGraph, the "map" phase fans out work to parallel nodes or subgraphs, and the "reduce" phase merges results back into a single state. There are two mechanisms:

1. **Static fan-out** — You know at build time how many branches you need (e.g., always 2 subgraphs). Use list returns from routing functions.
2. **Dynamic fan-out with `Send()`** — The number of branches depends on runtime data (e.g., one branch per document, per asset, per intent). Use `Send()` to create branches on the fly.

The key challenge: **state merge**. When parallel branches return, LangGraph must combine their outputs. Keys with reducers (like `add_messages`) merge automatically. Keys without reducers require careful design to avoid conflicts.

---

## 🔵 1.9.3 「 How It Works — Step by Step 」

```
╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║                    MAP-REDUCE FLOW IN LANGGRAPH                      ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║                                                                      ║
║  PATTERN 1: STATIC FAN-OUT (known at build time)                     ║
║  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────                   ║
║                                                                      ║
║  ┌────────┐     ┌──────────┐                                         ║
║  │ Router │────▶│ Returns   │                                        ║
║  │  Node  │     │ ["A","B"] │  ← List = parallel execution           ║
║  └────────┘     └──────────┘                                         ║
║                      │                                               ║
║              ┌───────┴───────┐                                       ║
║              ▼               ▼                                       ║
║         ┌────────┐     ┌────────┐    MAP PHASE                       ║
║         │ Node A │     │ Node B │    (parallel)                      ║
║         └───┬────┘     └───┬────┘                                    ║
║             │              │                                         ║
║             └──────┬───────┘                                         ║
║                    ▼                                                 ║
║              ┌──────────┐                                            ║
║              │  Reduce   │    REDUCE PHASE                           ║
║              │  (merge)  │    (state merge via reducers)             ║
║              └──────────┘                                            ║
║                                                                      ║
║  PATTERN 2: DYNAMIC FAN-OUT (runtime-determined)                     ║
║  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────                   ║
║                                                                      ║
║  ┌────────┐     ┌──────────────────────────────────┐                 ║
║  │ Router │────▶│ Returns [Send("Worker", item)    │                 ║
║  │  Node  │     │          for item in items]      │                 ║
║  └────────┘     └──────────────────────────────────┘                 ║
║                      │                                               ║
║         ┌────────────┼────────────┐                                  ║
║         ▼            ▼            ▼                                   ║
║    ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐   MAP PHASE                  ║
║    │Worker(1)│ │Worker(2)│ │Worker(N)│   (N parallel copies)         ║
║    └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘                               ║
║         │           │           │                                    ║
║         └───────────┼───────────┘                                    ║
║                     ▼                                                ║
║              ┌──────────┐                                            ║
║              │  Reduce   │   REDUCE PHASE                            ║
║              │  (merge)  │   (reducer combines N outputs)            ║
║              └──────────┘                                            ║
║                                                                      ║
║  STATE MERGE RULES:                                                  ║
║  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐             ║
║  │ Key with reducer (e.g., add_messages):               │            ║
║  │   Branch A: [msg1] + Branch B: [msg2] → [msg1, msg2]│            ║
║  │                                                      │            ║
║  │ Key WITHOUT reducer:                                  │            ║
║  │   Branch A: val_a + Branch B: val_b → ❌ CONFLICT    │            ║
║  │   Solution: Use list reducer or separate keys         │            ║
║  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘             ║
║                                                                      ║
║  Send() API:                                                         ║
║  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐             ║
║  │ from langgraph.graph import Send                      │            ║
║  │                                                      │            ║
║  │ def router(state):                                    │            ║
║  │     return [                                          │            ║
║  │         Send("worker_node", {"item": x, **state})     │            ║
║  │         for x in state["items"]                       │            ║
║  │     ]                                                 │            ║
║  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘             ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
```

**Key concepts:**
1. **Static fan-out**: Routing function returns `["NodeA", "NodeB"]` — fixed number of branches
2. **Dynamic fan-out**: Routing function returns `[Send("Node", state_i) for i in items]` — variable branches
3. **Reduce**: All branches must have edges to a common downstream node (or END)
4. **State merge**: Happens automatically using reducers; non-reducer keys cause conflicts if multiple branches write to them

---

## 🟢 1.9.4 「 Example 1: Static Map-Reduce — Multi-Source Context Gathering 」

<details>
<summary>📝 Click to expand full production code</summary>

```python
"""
Example 1: Static map-reduce — gather context from multiple data sources
in parallel and merge results.

Simulates fetching from a vector store, a SQL database, and a cache
simultaneously, then combining results for LLM consumption.
"""

from typing import TypedDict, Annotated, Dict, Any, List, Optional
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, START, END
import time
import operator


# ── Custom Reducer ─────────────────────────────────────────────────

def merge_context_dicts(existing: Dict[str, Any], update: Dict[str, Any]) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Reducer that merges context dictionaries from parallel branches."""
    merged = {**existing} if existing else {}
    merged.update(update)
    return merged


# ── State ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

class ContextState(TypedDict):
    query: str
    intent: str
    context: Annotated[Dict[str, Any], merge_context_dicts]
    results_count: Annotated[int, operator.add]


# ── Map Nodes (Data Source Workers) ────────────────────────────────

def fetch_vector_context(state: ContextState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Fetch relevant documents from vector store (OpenSearch/FAISS)."""
    query = state["query"]
    
    time.sleep(0.1)
    
    docs = [
        {"doc_id": "DOC-001", "content": f"Maintenance guide for query: {query[:50]}", "score": 0.92},
        {"doc_id": "DOC-002", "content": f"Troubleshooting steps for: {query[:50]}", "score": 0.87},
        {"doc_id": "DOC-003", "content": f"Safety procedures related to: {query[:50]}", "score": 0.81},
    ]
    
    formatted = "\n".join(
        f"Doc: {d['doc_id']} | Score: {d['score']:.2f} | {d['content']}"
        for d in docs
    )
    
    return {
        "context": {"vector_docs": formatted, "vector_count": len(docs)},
        "results_count": len(docs),
    }


def fetch_sql_context(state: ContextState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Fetch structured data from SQL database (Snowflake)."""
    intent = state.get("intent", "general")
    
    time.sleep(0.15)
    
    if intent == "maintenance_history":
        data = {
            "last_service": "2024-01-15",
            "service_count": 12,
            "avg_interval_days": 45,
            "critical_issues": 2,
        }
    elif intent == "workorder_briefing":
        data = {
            "workorder_id": "WO-12345",
            "priority": "HIGH",
            "assigned_crew": "Alpha Team",
            "estimated_hours": 4.5,
        }
    else:
        data = {"note": "No structured context for this intent"}
    
    formatted = "\n".join(f"  {k}: {v}" for k, v in data.items())
    
    return {
        "context": {"sql_data": formatted, "sql_source": "snowflake"},
        "results_count": len(data),
    }


def fetch_cache_context(state: ContextState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Check cache for recent similar queries."""
    query = state["query"]
    
    time.sleep(0.05)
    
    cache_hit = len(query) % 2 == 0
    
    if cache_hit:
        cached = f"Cached response for similar query (similarity: 0.95): Previous answer available"
    else:
        cached = "No cache hit"
    
    return {
        "context": {"cache_result": cached, "cache_hit": cache_hit},
        "results_count": 1 if cache_hit else 0,
    }


# ── Reduce Node ───────────────────────────────────────────────────

def combine_context(state: ContextState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Combine all gathered context into a final merged context."""
    ctx = state.get("context", {})
    total = state.get("results_count", 0)
    
    summary = (
        f"Context Summary ({total} total results):\n"
        f"  Vector: {ctx.get('vector_count', 0)} docs\n"
        f"  SQL: {ctx.get('sql_source', 'none')}\n"
        f"  Cache: {'HIT' if ctx.get('cache_hit') else 'MISS'}\n"
    )
    
    ctx["summary"] = summary
    return {"context": ctx}


# ── Router ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def fan_out_to_sources(state: ContextState) -> List[str]:
    """Fan out to all three data sources in parallel."""
    return ["FetchVector", "FetchSQL", "FetchCache"]


# ── Build Graph ──────────────────────────────────────────────────

def build_context_gatherer():
    """Build a static map-reduce graph for multi-source context gathering."""
    graph = StateGraph(ContextState)
    
    graph.add_node("Prepare", lambda state: {"intent": state.get("intent", "general")})
    graph.add_node("FetchVector", fetch_vector_context)
    graph.add_node("FetchSQL", fetch_sql_context)
    graph.add_node("FetchCache", fetch_cache_context)
    graph.add_node("Combine", combine_context)
    
    graph.add_edge(START, "Prepare")
    graph.add_conditional_edges("Prepare", fan_out_to_sources)
    
    graph.add_edge("FetchVector", "Combine")
    graph.add_edge("FetchSQL", "Combine")
    graph.add_edge("FetchCache", "Combine")
    
    graph.add_edge("Combine", END)
    
    return graph.compile()


# ── Usage ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

if __name__ == "__main__":
    gatherer = build_context_gatherer()
    
    start = time.time()
    result = gatherer.invoke({
        "query": "What maintenance is needed for pump P-101?",
        "intent": "maintenance_history",
    })
    elapsed = time.time() - start
    
    print(result["context"]["summary"])
    print(f"Total results: {result['results_count']}")
    print(f"Elapsed: {elapsed:.2f}s (sequential would be ~0.30s)")
```

</details>

**What this demonstrates:**
- Static fan-out: `fan_out_to_sources` returns `["FetchVector", "FetchSQL", "FetchCache"]`
- Custom reducer `merge_context_dicts` safely merges dictionaries from parallel branches
- `operator.add` reducer for `results_count` sums across branches
- All three fetch nodes converge at `Combine` (reduce phase)
- Wall-clock time ~0.15s (max branch) vs ~0.30s (sum of all branches) — 2× speedup

---

## 🟢 1.9.5 「 Example 2: Dynamic Map-Reduce with Send() — Per-Document Processing 」

<details>
<summary>📝 Click to expand full production code</summary>

```python
"""
Example 2: Dynamic map-reduce using Send() API.

Processes a variable number of documents in parallel — the count
is determined at runtime, not build time. Each document gets its
own parallel branch via Send().
"""

from typing import TypedDict, Annotated, Dict, Any, List
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, START, END, Send
import hashlib
import operator


# ── Reducer ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def append_list(existing: List, update: List) -> List:
    """Reducer that appends lists from parallel branches."""
    return (existing or []) + (update or [])


# ── State ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

class MapReduceState(TypedDict):
    documents: List[Dict[str, str]]
    processed: Annotated[List[Dict[str, Any]], append_list]
    total_chars: Annotated[int, operator.add]
    error_count: Annotated[int, operator.add]


class WorkerState(TypedDict):
    """State for each parallel worker — receives one document."""
    document: Dict[str, str]
    processed: Annotated[List[Dict[str, Any]], append_list]
    total_chars: Annotated[int, operator.add]
    error_count: Annotated[int, operator.add]


# ── Worker Node ───────────────────────────────────────────────────

def process_document(state: WorkerState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Process a single document — runs in parallel via Send()."""
    doc = state["document"]
    doc_id = doc.get("id", "unknown")
    content = doc.get("content", "")
    
    try:
        word_count = len(content.split())
        char_count = len(content)
        content_hash = hashlib.md5(content.encode()).hexdigest()[:8]
        
        sentences = [s.strip() for s in content.split(".") if s.strip()]
        
        keywords = set()
        for word in content.lower().split():
            cleaned = word.strip(".,!?;:")
            if len(cleaned) > 4 and cleaned.isalpha():
                keywords.add(cleaned)
        
        result = {
            "doc_id": doc_id,
            "word_count": word_count,
            "char_count": char_count,
            "sentence_count": len(sentences),
            "content_hash": content_hash,
            "top_keywords": sorted(keywords)[:5],
            "status": "success",
        }
        
        return {
            "processed": [result],
            "total_chars": char_count,
            "error_count": 0,
        }
        
    except Exception as e:
        return {
            "processed": [{"doc_id": doc_id, "status": "error", "error": str(e)}],
            "total_chars": 0,
            "error_count": 1,
        }


# ── Router with Send() ───────────────────────────────────────────

def fan_out_documents(state: MapReduceState) -> List[Send]:
    """Create one Send() per document for dynamic parallel processing."""
    documents = state.get("documents", [])
    
    return [
        Send("ProcessDocument", {
            "document": doc,
            "processed": [],
            "total_chars": 0,
            "error_count": 0,
        })
        for doc in documents
    ]


# ── Reduce Node ──────────────────────────────────────────────────

def aggregate_results(state: MapReduceState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Aggregate results from all parallel document processors."""
    processed = state.get("processed", [])
    total_chars = state.get("total_chars", 0)
    errors = state.get("error_count", 0)
    
    successful = [p for p in processed if p.get("status") == "success"]
    
    all_keywords = set()
    for p in successful:
        all_keywords.update(p.get("top_keywords", []))
    
    summary = {
        "doc_id": "AGGREGATE",
        "total_documents": len(processed),
        "successful": len(successful),
        "errors": errors,
        "total_chars": total_chars,
        "total_words": sum(p.get("word_count", 0) for p in successful),
        "global_keywords": sorted(all_keywords)[:20],
        "status": "complete",
    }
    
    return {"processed": [summary]}


# ── Build Graph ──────────────────────────────────────────────────

def build_document_processor():
    """Build dynamic map-reduce graph for document processing."""
    graph = StateGraph(MapReduceState)
    
    graph.add_node("ProcessDocument", process_document)
    graph.add_node("Aggregate", aggregate_results)
    
    graph.add_conditional_edges(START, fan_out_documents)
    
    graph.add_edge("ProcessDocument", "Aggregate")
    graph.add_edge("Aggregate", END)
    
    return graph.compile()


# ── Usage ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

if __name__ == "__main__":
    processor = build_document_processor()
    
    documents = [
        {"id": "DOC-001", "content": "The centrifugal pump requires bearing replacement. Vibration levels have exceeded the threshold of 4.5mm/s. Maintenance crew should inspect the shaft alignment during the next scheduled downtime."},
        {"id": "DOC-002", "content": "Motor winding insulation test results show degradation. Megger reading dropped from 500 MOhm to 150 MOhm over six months. Recommend rewinding during planned outage in Q2."},
        {"id": "DOC-003", "content": "Valve actuator response time has increased from 1.2.5 seconds to 4.1 seconds. Hydraulic fluid analysis shows contamination. Replace fluid and recalibrate actuator."},
        {"id": "DOC-004", "content": "Cooling tower fan blade inspection complete. Two blades show fatigue cracks at root. Replacement parts on order. Estimated delivery three weeks."},
        {"id": "DOC-005", "content": "Compressor discharge pressure trending upward. Filter differential pressure at 85% of maximum. Schedule filter replacement before reaching alarm setpoint."},
    ]
    
    result = processor.invoke({"documents": documents})
    
    for item in result["processed"]:
        if item["doc_id"] == "AGGREGATE":
            print(f"\n=== Aggregate Results ===")
            print(f"Documents processed: {item['total_documents']}")
            print(f"Successful: {item['successful']}, Errors: {item['errors']}")
            print(f"Total chars: {item['total_chars']}, Total words: {item['total_words']}")
            print(f"Global keywords: {', '.join(item['global_keywords'][:10])}")
        else:
            print(f"[{item['doc_id']}] {item['status']} — {item.get('word_count', 0)} words, hash: {item.get('content_hash', 'N/A')}")
```

</details>

**What this demonstrates:**
- **Dynamic fan-out**: `fan_out_documents` returns `[Send("ProcessDocument", state) for doc in docs]`
- Variable number of branches (5 documents → 5 parallel workers)
- `WorkerState` receives one document per branch
- `append_list` reducer safely merges result lists from all branches
- `operator.add` reducer sums `total_chars` and `error_count` across branches
- Aggregate node runs after all workers complete

---

## 🟢 1.9.6 「 Example 3: Hierarchical Map-Reduce — Multi-Asset Context Assembly 」

<details>
<summary>📝 Click to expand full production code</summary>

```python
"""
Example 3: Hierarchical map-reduce — processes multiple assets,
each requiring both vector and SQL context (two-level fan-out).

Level 1: Fan out per asset
Level 2: Each asset fans out to vector + SQL sources
Reduce: Merge per-asset results, then merge all assets
"""

from typing import TypedDict, Annotated, Dict, Any, List, Optional
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, START, END, Send
import operator


# ── Reducers ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def merge_asset_contexts(existing: List[Dict], update: List[Dict]) -> List[Dict]:
    """Merge asset context lists from parallel branches."""
    return (existing or []) + (update or [])


# ── States ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

class AssetWorkerState(TypedDict):
    """State for processing a single asset's context."""
    asset_id: str
    tenant_id: str
    query: str
    asset_contexts: Annotated[List[Dict[str, Any]], merge_asset_contexts]


class OrchestratorState(TypedDict):
    """Top-level state managing all assets."""
    asset_ids: List[str]
    tenant_id: str
    query: str
    asset_contexts: Annotated[List[Dict[str, Any]], merge_asset_contexts]
    final_context: str


# ── Asset Worker Nodes ────────────────────────────────────────────

def gather_asset_vector_context(state: AssetWorkerState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Fetch vector documents for a specific asset."""
    asset_id = state["asset_id"]
    query = state["query"]
    
    docs = [
        f"[{asset_id}] Maintenance manual section 1.4.2: Regular inspection schedule",
        f"[{asset_id}] Service bulletin SB-2024-03: Bearing replacement procedure",
        f"[{asset_id}] Safety protocol SP-101: Lockout/tagout requirements",
    ]
    
    return {
        "asset_contexts": [{
            "asset_id": asset_id,
            "source": "vector",
            "documents": docs,
            "doc_count": len(docs),
        }],
    }


def gather_asset_sql_context(state: AssetWorkerState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Fetch SQL data for a specific asset."""
    asset_id = state["asset_id"]
    tenant_id = state["tenant_id"]
    
    history = {
        "asset_id": asset_id,
        "tenant_id": tenant_id,
        "last_service": "2024-02-15",
        "total_workorders": 24,
        "open_workorders": 2,
        "mean_time_between_failures": "45 days",
        "criticality": "HIGH",
    }
    
    return {
        "asset_contexts": [{
            "asset_id": asset_id,
            "source": "sql",
            "history": history,
        }],
    }


def merge_asset_data(state: AssetWorkerState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Merge vector + SQL context for a single asset."""
    contexts = state.get("asset_contexts", [])
    asset_id = state["asset_id"]
    
    vector_ctx = next((c for c in contexts if c.get("source") == "vector"), {})
    sql_ctx = next((c for c in contexts if c.get("source") == "sql"), {})
    
    merged = {
        "asset_id": asset_id,
        "source": "merged",
        "documents": vector_ctx.get("documents", []),
        "history": sql_ctx.get("history", {}),
        "doc_count": vector_ctx.get("doc_count", 0),
    }
    
    return {"asset_contexts": [merged]}


# ── Build Per-Asset Subgraph ─────────────────────────────────────

def fan_out_asset_sources(state: AssetWorkerState) -> List[str]:
    """Fan out to vector and SQL sources for this asset."""
    return ["AssetVector", "AssetSQL"]


def build_asset_subgraph():
    """Build subgraph for processing a single asset."""
    graph = StateGraph(AssetWorkerState)
    
    graph.add_node("AssetVector", gather_asset_vector_context)
    graph.add_node("AssetSQL", gather_asset_sql_context)
    graph.add_node("MergeAsset", merge_asset_data)
    
    graph.add_conditional_edges(START, fan_out_asset_sources)
    graph.add_edge("AssetVector", "MergeAsset")
    graph.add_edge("AssetSQL", "MergeAsset")
    graph.add_edge("MergeAsset", END)
    
    return graph.compile()


# ── Orchestrator ──────────────────────────────────────────────────

def fan_out_assets(state: OrchestratorState) -> List[Send]:
    """Create one Send() per asset for parallel processing."""
    asset_ids = state.get("asset_ids", [])
    
    return [
        Send("ProcessAsset", {
            "asset_id": asset_id,
            "tenant_id": state["tenant_id"],
            "query": state["query"],
            "asset_contexts": [],
        })
        for asset_id in asset_ids
    ]


def build_final_context(state: OrchestratorState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Build final merged context from all asset results."""
    contexts = state.get("asset_contexts", [])
    
    merged_only = [c for c in contexts if c.get("source") == "merged"]
    
    lines = [f"Context for {len(merged_only)} assets:\n"]
    for ctx in merged_only:
        aid = ctx["asset_id"]
        doc_count = ctx.get("doc_count", 0)
        history = ctx.get("history", {})
        
        lines.append(f"Asset {aid}:")
        lines.append(f"  Documents: {doc_count} retrieved")
        lines.append(f"  Last service: {history.get('last_service', 'N/A')}")
        lines.append(f"  MTBF: {history.get('mean_time_between_failures', 'N/A')}")
        lines.append(f"  Criticality: {history.get('criticality', 'N/A')}")
        lines.append("")
    
    return {"final_context": "\n".join(lines)}


def build_multi_asset_processor():
    """Build hierarchical map-reduce graph."""
    graph = StateGraph(OrchestratorState)
    
    asset_subgraph = build_asset_subgraph()
    
    graph.add_node("ProcessAsset", asset_subgraph)
    graph.add_node("BuildContext", build_final_context)
    
    graph.add_conditional_edges(START, fan_out_assets)
    graph.add_edge("ProcessAsset", "BuildContext")
    graph.add_edge("BuildContext", END)
    
    return graph.compile()


# ── Usage ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

if __name__ == "__main__":
    processor = build_multi_asset_processor()
    
    result = processor.invoke({
        "asset_ids": ["PUMP-101", "MOTOR-202", "VALVE-303"],
        "tenant_id": "smg",
        "query": "What maintenance is overdue?",
    })
    
    print(result["final_context"])
    print(f"\nTotal context entries: {len(result['asset_contexts'])}")
```

</details>

**What this demonstrates:**
- **Two-level map-reduce**: Orchestrator fans out per asset → each asset subgraph fans out to vector + SQL
- `Send()` for dynamic per-asset parallelism at orchestrator level
- Static fan-out (`["AssetVector", "AssetSQL"]`) at per-asset subgraph level
- Custom `merge_asset_contexts` reducer safely combines results across all branches
- Pattern mirrors DC-Copilot's multi-asset context gathering architecture

---

## 🟢 1.9.7 「 How to Implement (Production Guide) 」

### Step 1: Identify Map-Reduce Opportunities

```python
"""
Look for these patterns in your code:

1. Sequential loops doing independent work:
   for item in items:
       result = process(item)  # Each iteration is independent
   
2. Multiple data sources fetched sequentially:
   vector_ctx = fetch_vector(query)
   sql_ctx = fetch_sql(query)
   cache_ctx = check_cache(query)
   
3. Aggregation after parallel work:
   results = [process(item) for item in items]
   final = aggregate(results)
"""
```

### Step 2: Choose Static vs Dynamic Fan-Out

```python
# STATIC: Known number of branches at build time
# Use when: Always the same sources (e.g., vector + SQL + cache)
def route_to_sources(state) -> List[str]:
    return ["FetchVector", "FetchSQL", "FetchCache"]

# DYNAMIC: Variable number of branches at runtime
# Use when: Number depends on input (e.g., N documents, N assets)
from langgraph.graph import Send

def route_to_workers(state) -> List[Send]:
    return [
        Send("Worker", {"item": item, **base_state})
        for item in state["items"]
    ]
```

### Step 3: Design Reducers for Safe Merging

```python
import operator
from typing import Annotated, List, Dict, Any


def merge_dicts(a: Dict, b: Dict) -> Dict:
    """Reducer: merge dictionaries (later values override)."""
    merged = {**a} if a else {}
    merged.update(b)
    return merged


def concat_lists(a: List, b: List) -> List:
    """Reducer: concatenate lists from parallel branches."""
    return (a or []) + (b or [])


class MapReduceState(TypedDict):
    items: List[str]                                        # Input — no reducer needed
    results: Annotated[List[Dict], concat_lists]            # Append from each branch
    total_count: Annotated[int, operator.add]               # Sum from each branch
    context: Annotated[Dict[str, Any], merge_dicts]         # Merge from each branch
```

### Step 4: Handle Errors in Map Workers

```python
def safe_worker(state: WorkerState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Worker with error handling — never crashes the fan-out."""
    try:
        result = do_expensive_work(state["item"])
        return {
            "results": [{"item": state["item"], "status": "success", "data": result}],
            "error_count": 0,
        }
    except Exception as e:
        return {
            "results": [{"item": state["item"], "status": "error", "error": str(e)}],
            "error_count": 1,
        }
```

### Step 5: Add Timeouts and Limits

```python
import asyncio
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, TimeoutError


def worker_with_timeout(state: WorkerState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Worker with timeout protection."""
    MAX_WORKER_SECONDS = 30
    
    with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=1) as executor:
        future = executor.submit(do_work, state["item"])
        try:
            result = future.result(timeout=MAX_WORKER_SECONDS)
            return {"results": [result]}
        except TimeoutError:
            return {"results": [{"status": "timeout", "item": state["item"]}]}


def fan_out_with_limit(state) -> List[Send]:
    """Limit maximum parallel branches to prevent resource exhaustion."""
    MAX_PARALLEL = 10
    items = state["items"][:MAX_PARALLEL]
    
    if len(state["items"]) > MAX_PARALLEL:
        print(f"Warning: truncated from {len(state['items'])} to {MAX_PARALLEL} items")
    
    return [Send("Worker", {"item": item}) for item in items]
```

---

## 🔵 1.9.8 「 Comparison Table (Approaches) 」

| Approach | Branch Count | Use Case | State Merge | Complexity |
|----------|-------------|----------|-------------|------------|
| **Static fan-out (list return)** | Fixed at build time | Known data sources (vector + SQL + cache) | Reducers on shared keys | Low |
| **Dynamic fan-out (Send())** | Runtime-determined | Per-document, per-asset processing | Reducers + worker state | Medium |
| **Subgraph fan-out** | Fixed subgraphs | Complex multi-node parallel pipelines | State isolation | Medium |
| **ThreadPoolExecutor (manual)** | Manual threading | Non-LangGraph parallel work | Manual merge | High |
| **asyncio.gather** | Async tasks | I/O-bound parallel calls | Manual merge | Medium |
| **Sequential loop** | 1 (no parallelism) | Debugging, < 3 items, dependencies | N/A | Lowest |
| **Nested map-reduce** | Multi-level | Hierarchical data (assets → sources) | Multi-level reducers | High |

**Recommendation:** Use static fan-out for fixed-source gathering, `Send()` for variable-count processing, and sequential for anything with < 3 items or inter-item dependencies.

---

## 🟠 1.9.9 「 When and Where to Use This Pattern 」

### Use Map-Reduce When:

| Scenario | Pattern | Expected Speedup |
|----------|---------|-------------------|
| **Multi-source context gathering** | Static fan-out | 2-5× (limited by slowest source) |
| **Per-document processing** | Dynamic Send() | Near-linear with document count |
| **Multi-asset work orders** | Dynamic Send() | Near-linear with asset count |
| **Batch embedding generation** | Dynamic Send() | Limited by API rate limits |
| **Parallel validation checks** | Static fan-out | 2-4× (limited by slowest check) |
| **Multi-model inference** | Static fan-out | 2-3× (limited by slowest model) |

### Skip Map-Reduce When:

| Scenario | Reason |
|----------|--------|
| **< 3 items to process** | Overhead exceeds benefit |
| **Items depend on each other** | Can't parallelize dependent work |
| **Single data source** | Nothing to fan out |
| **Rate-limited API calls** | Parallel requests hit rate limits faster |
| **Memory-constrained** | Each branch holds state copy |
| **Debugging active issue** | Sequential is easier to trace |

---

## ✅ 1.9.9b 「 Do's and Don'ts 」

| ✅ Do | ❌ Don't |
|-------|---------|
| Use reducers on every key that parallel branches write to | Write to non-reducer keys from parallel branches |
| Limit max parallel branches (e.g., 10-20) | Fan out to 1000+ branches without limits |
| Handle errors in each worker (return error status) | Let worker exceptions crash the entire fan-out |
| Use `Send()` for runtime-variable branch counts | Use `Send()` for fixed-count fan-outs (use list return instead) |
| Add timeouts to long-running workers | Let workers run indefinitely |
| Test workers independently before composing | Only test the full map-reduce graph |
| Monitor reducer memory (list growth) | Let reducers accumulate unbounded data |
| Use `operator.add` for numeric aggregation | Write custom reducers for simple addition |

---

## ⚪ 1.9.10 「 Pros and Cons 」

| Dimension | Pro | Con |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Latency** | Wall-clock time = slowest branch, not sum | Minimum overhead ~2ms per branch |
| **Throughput** | Process N items in time of 1 | Memory scales with N (each branch holds state) |
| **Error isolation** | One branch failure doesn't kill others | Must handle partial results in reduce phase |
| **Code clarity** | Clean separation of map/reduce logic | Reducer design requires careful thought |
| **Debugging** | Each branch has its own trace | Parallel interleaving makes logs harder to read |
| **Testing** | Workers testable independently | Integration tests need mock parallel execution |
| **State management** | Reducers handle merge automatically | Non-obvious merge semantics (append vs. replace) |
| **Scalability** | Linear speedup for independent work | Diminishing returns past CPU/connection pool limits |

---

## 🟠 1.9.11 「 The DC-Copilot Connection 」

DC-Copilot implements map-reduce patterns at **two architectural levels**: parallel subgraph fan-out and per-asset context pre-computation.

### Level 1: Parallel Subgraph Fan-Out

The parent graph in `state_machine.py` (line 39-47) implements static fan-out:

```python
# state_machine.py — route_after_profanity (line 39)
def route_after_profanity(state):
    if state.early_exit:
        return "__end__"
    return ["CopilotFlow", "SummarizationSubgraph"]  # ← Static fan-out
```

This is a **two-branch static map-reduce**:
- **Map**: `CopilotFlow` (classify → context → fanout → LLM) runs in parallel with `SummarizationSubgraph` (summarize conversation history)
- **Reduce**: Both branches converge at `END` — the `add_messages` reducer merges their message outputs, while state isolation (different TypedDicts) prevents key conflicts

### Level 2: Context Gathering — Vector Retrieval

`ContextGatheringNode` in `routing.py` (line 86) gathers vector context from OpenSearch:

```python
# routing.py — ContextGatheringNode._execute (line 101)
def _execute(self, state, config):
    intents = state.intent or []
    if any(intent in self._vector_context_intents for intent in intents):
        vector_updates = self._gather_vector_context(state, cfg)
        updates.update(vector_updates)
    return updates
```

Currently this runs vector retrieval sequentially per intent. The `_vector_context_intents` set (from `IntentType.get_vector_context_intents()`) determines which intents require document retrieval vs. which can skip it (e.g., greetings, meta answers).

### Level 3: Pre-Computed Snowflake Context (Scatter-Gather)

`context_precompute.py` (line 21) implements a **per-asset scatter-gather pattern**:

```python
# context_precompute.py — precompute_all_context (line 21)
def precompute_all_context(wo_row, workorder_id, tenant_id, asset_ids, snowflake_client, logger):
    # Scatter: compute briefing + history for each asset
    briefings = _compute_briefings(wo_row, workorder_id, tenant_id, asset_ids, ...)
    asset_contexts = _compute_maintenance_history(workorder_id, tenant_id, asset_ids, ...)
    
    # Gather: merge per-asset results into workorder_context list
    workorder_context = []
    for idx, asset_id in enumerate(asset_ids):
        briefing = briefings[idx] if idx < len(briefings) else ""
        asset_ctx = asset_contexts[idx] if idx < len(asset_contexts) else ""
        workorder_context.append({
            "asset_id": asset_id,
            "snowflake_context": {
                "workorder_briefing": briefing or "No briefing available",
                "asset_context": asset_ctx or "No context available",
            },
        })
    return {"workorder_context": workorder_context, "context_preloaded": True, ...}
```

This runs at `chatInitialize` time (before the chat graph), pre-computing Snowflake-based context for **all assets** so that `ContextGatheringNode` in the chat graph only needs to fetch vector context (OpenSearch), which depends on the user's current query.

### Architecture Pattern: Two-Phase Context Assembly

```
╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║          DC-COPILOT TWO-PHASE MAP-REDUCE                     ║
║                                                              ║
║  PHASE 1: chatInitialize (pre-compute, per-asset)            ║
║  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐                ║
║  │  for asset_id in asset_ids:              │                ║
║  │    briefing  ← Snowflake (wo_row + time) │  ← Map        ║
║  │    history   ← Snowflake (maintenance)   │               ║
║  │  workorder_context ← merge all           │  ← Reduce     ║
║  │  Store in checkpoint (context_preloaded)  │               ║
║  └──────────────────────────────────────────┘                ║
║                       │                                      ║
║                       ▼                                      ║
║  PHASE 2: /chat graph (runtime, per-query)                   ║
║  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐                ║
║  │  Parallel fan-out:                        │               ║
║  │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌───────────────────┐ │               ║
║  │  │ CopilotFlow  │  │ Summarization     │ │  ← Map        ║
║  │  │              │  │ Subgraph          │ │               ║
║  │  │ ClassifyInt. │  │                   │ │               ║
║  │  │ → ContextG.  │  │ Summarize history │ │               ║
║  │  │   (vector    │  │                   │ │               ║
║  │  │    only!)    │  │                   │ │               ║
║  │  │ → Fanout     │  │                   │ │               ║
║  │  │ → LLMInvoke  │  │                   │ │               ║
║  │  └──────┬───────┘  └────────┬──────────┘ │               ║
║  │         └────────┬──────────┘            │               ║
║  │                  ▼                        │               ║
║  │                 END (state merge)         │  ← Reduce     ║
║  └──────────────────────────────────────────┘                ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
```

### Optimization Opportunity

The current per-asset loops in `_compute_briefings` and `_compute_maintenance_history` run sequentially. For work orders with 5+ assets, converting these to `ThreadPoolExecutor` or `Send()`-based parallel processing could reduce `chatInitialize` latency by 3-5×.

---

## 🔴 1.9.12 「 Anti-Patterns Gallery 」

### Anti-Pattern 1: No Reducer on Parallel-Written Keys

```python
# ❌ WRONG: Two parallel branches write to "result" without a reducer
class BrokenState(TypedDict):
    result: str  # ← No reducer! Second branch overwrites first.

def branch_a(state) -> Dict:
    return {"result": "analysis complete"}

def branch_b(state) -> Dict:
    return {"result": "validation complete"}

# Only one result survives — which one is non-deterministic!

# ✅ FIX: Use a list with a reducer
class FixedState(TypedDict):
    results: Annotated[List[str], operator.add]

def branch_a(state) -> Dict:
    return {"results": ["analysis complete"]}

def branch_b(state) -> Dict:
    return {"results": ["validation complete"]}
# Both results preserved: ["analysis complete", "validation complete"]
```

### Anti-Pattern 2: Unbounded Send() Fan-Out

```python
# ❌ WRONG: No limit on parallel branches
def fan_out(state) -> List[Send]:
    return [Send("Worker", {"doc": d}) for d in state["documents"]]
    # If documents has 10,000 items → 10,000 parallel branches
    # Memory explosion, thread pool exhaustion, OOM kill

# ✅ FIX: Cap and batch
MAX_PARALLEL = 20

def fan_out_limited(state) -> List[Send]:
    docs = state["documents"][:MAX_PARALLEL]
    return [Send("Worker", {"doc": d}) for d in docs]
```

### Anti-Pattern 3: Dependencies Between Map Workers

```python
# ❌ WRONG: Worker B depends on Worker A's output
def worker_a(state) -> Dict:
    return {"shared_cache": {"key": "value"}}  # Worker B needs this

def worker_b(state) -> Dict:
    cache = state.get("shared_cache", {})  # ← Empty! A hasn't finished yet
    return {"result": cache.get("key", "MISSING")}

# ✅ FIX: Make workers independent, or run sequentially
# Option A: Each worker fetches its own data
# Option B: Pre-compute shared data before fan-out
def prepare(state) -> Dict:
    return {"shared_cache": compute_cache()}

# Then fan out after Prepare node
```

### Anti-Pattern 4: Ignoring Errors in Reduce Phase

```python
# ❌ WRONG: Reduce assumes all branches succeeded
def reduce(state) -> Dict:
    results = state["results"]
    total = sum(r["value"] for r in results)  # ← KeyError if any branch errored!
    return {"total": total}

# ✅ FIX: Filter and handle errors
def reduce(state) -> Dict:
    results = state["results"]
    successful = [r for r in results if r.get("status") == "success"]
    errors = [r for r in results if r.get("status") == "error"]
    
    total = sum(r["value"] for r in successful)
    return {
        "total": total,
        "success_count": len(successful),
        "error_count": len(errors),
    }
```

---

## ⚪ 1.9.13 「 Performance Benchmark Table 」

| Metric | Sequential | Static Fan-Out (3) | Dynamic Send() (10) | Dynamic Send() (50) |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Wall-clock (each 200ms)** | 600ms | ~210ms | ~215ms | ~250ms |
| **Wall-clock (each 1s)** | 3s | ~1.05s | ~1.1s | ~1.3s |
| **Memory per branch** | Baseline | +~0.5MB each | +~0.5MB each | +~0.5MB each |
| **Total memory (50 items)** | ~50MB | N/A (fixed 3) | ~55MB | ~75MB |
| **Reducer overhead** | None | ~0.1ms | ~0.5ms | ~2ms |
| **Thread pool contention** | None | Minimal | Moderate (10 threads) | High (50 threads) |
| **Error blast radius** | All stop | 1 of 3 | 1 of 10 | 1 of 50 |
| **Debuggability** | Easy | Moderate | Harder | Hardest |

**Key finding:** Parallel speedup is near-linear up to ~10-20 branches. Beyond 20, thread pool contention and memory overhead start eroding gains. For 50+ items, batch into groups of 10-20 and run batches sequentially.

---

## 🔵 1.9.14 「 Decision Flowchart 」

```
                    ┌──────────────────────────────┐
                    │ Do you have independent work  │
                    │ that can run in parallel?      │
                    └──────────────┬────────────────┘
                                   │
                         ┌─────────┴─────────┐
                         │ No                │ Yes
                         ▼                   ▼
                  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────────────────┐
                  │ Use          │  │ Is the number of branches │
                  │ sequential   │  │ known at build time?      │
                  │ processing   │  └──────────────┬────────────┘
                  └──────────────┘                 │
                                         ┌─────────┴─────────┐
                                         │ Yes               │ No
                                         ▼                   ▼
                                 ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐
                                 │ Static       │  │ How many items   │
                                 │ fan-out      │  │ at runtime?      │
                                 │ (list return)│  └────────┬─────────┘
                                 └──────────────┘           │
                                                  ┌─────────┴──────────┐
                                                  │ ≤ 20              │ > 20
                                                  ▼                   ▼
                                          ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐
                                          │ Dynamic      │  │ Batch into       │
                                          │ Send()       │  │ groups of 10-20  │
                                          │ fan-out      │  │ + sequential     │
                                          └──────────────┘  │ batch processing │
                                                            └──────────────────┘
                                                  │
                                                  ▼
                                          ┌──────────────────┐
                                          │ Do branches need  │
                                          │ internal multi-   │
                                          │ step processing?  │
                                          └────────┬─────────┘
                                                   │
                                         ┌─────────┴─────────┐
                                         │ No                │ Yes
                                         ▼                   ▼
                                  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐
                                  │ Single worker│  │ Use subgraph per │
                                  │ function     │  │ branch (compiled │
                                  │              │  │ graph as worker) │
                                  └──────────────┘  └──────────────────┘
```

## 🟢 1.9.15 「 Migration Path 」

### Step 0 → Identify Sequential Bottleneck

```python
# Profile your current sequential code
import time

# Current: sequential loop
start = time.time()
results = []
for asset_id in asset_ids:  # 5 assets, each takes ~1s
    ctx = fetch_context(asset_id)
    results.append(ctx)
elapsed = time.time() - start
# elapsed ≈ 5.0s — ripe for map-reduce

# Target: parallel with map-reduce
# expected ≈ 1.1s (slowest branch + overhead)
```

### Step 1 → Define Reducer and State

```python
from typing import TypedDict, Annotated, List, Dict, Any
import operator

def concat(a: List, b: List) -> List:
    return (a or []) + (b or [])

class ParallelState(TypedDict):
    asset_ids: List[str]
    results: Annotated[List[Dict[str, Any]], concat]
    error_count: Annotated[int, operator.add]
```

### Step 2 → Create Worker Node

```python
def process_asset(state) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Worker that processes one asset. Must handle its own errors."""
    asset_id = state["asset_id"]
    try:
        ctx = fetch_context(asset_id)
        return {"results": [{"asset_id": asset_id, "context": ctx, "status": "ok"}], "error_count": 0}
    except Exception as e:
        return {"results": [{"asset_id": asset_id, "error": str(e), "status": "error"}], "error_count": 1}
```

### Step 3 → Wire Up Map-Reduce Graph

```python
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, START, END, Send

def fan_out(state) -> List[Send]:
    return [
        Send("ProcessAsset", {"asset_id": aid, "results": [], "error_count": 0})
        for aid in state["asset_ids"]
    ]

def aggregate(state) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    ok = [r for r in state["results"] if r["status"] == "ok"]
    return {"results": [{"summary": f"{len(ok)} assets processed"}]}

graph = StateGraph(ParallelState)
graph.add_node("ProcessAsset", process_asset)
graph.add_node("Aggregate", aggregate)
graph.add_conditional_edges(START, fan_out)
graph.add_edge("ProcessAsset", "Aggregate")
graph.add_edge("Aggregate", END)

compiled = graph.compile()
```

---

## 💡 1.9.16 「 Interview / Viva Q&A 」

**Q1: What is the difference between static fan-out and dynamic fan-out with `Send()` in LangGraph?**

A1: **Static fan-out** uses a routing function that returns a list of node names (e.g., `return ["NodeA", "NodeB"]`). The branches are fixed at graph build time — always the same nodes. **Dynamic fan-out** uses `Send()` to create branches at runtime: `return [Send("Worker", state_i) for i in items]`. Each `Send()` creates a separate invocation of the same node with different input state. Use static when you have a known set of parallel tasks; use `Send()` when the number of branches depends on runtime data.

**Q2: How does LangGraph merge state from parallel branches?**

A2: Through **reducers**. Each state key can have a reducer function via `Annotated[Type, reducer]`. When parallel branches complete:
- Keys WITH reducers: the reducer is called with the existing value and each branch's update (e.g., `operator.add` for integers, `add_messages` for message lists, custom functions for dicts/lists)
- Keys WITHOUT reducers: LangGraph raises `INVALID_CONCURRENT_GRAPH_UPDATE` if multiple branches write different values to the same key
- Keys written by only ONE branch: the single value is used, no conflict

**Q3: How do you handle errors in map workers without crashing the entire graph?**

A3: Each worker should wrap its logic in try/except and return an error status instead of raising. Use a list reducer on the results key so error entries and success entries both accumulate. In the reduce phase, filter by status: `successful = [r for r in results if r["status"] == "success"]`. This way, 4 out of 5 workers succeeding still produces useful output. The `error_count` key with `operator.add` reducer tracks total failures.

**Q4: What are the practical limits of `Send()` parallelism?**

A4: There are three practical limits: (1) **Memory** — each branch holds a copy of its input state; at 1MB per branch, 1000 branches = 1GB RAM. (2) **Thread pool** — Python's GIL limits CPU-bound parallelism; for I/O-bound work (API calls, DB queries), 10-20 branches work well. (3) **External rate limits** — if workers call rate-limited APIs, more branches means hitting limits faster. Best practice: cap at 10-20 parallel branches and batch beyond that.

**Q5: Can you nest map-reduce — i.e., have a `Send()` fan-out inside a subgraph that's itself part of a fan-out?**

A5: Yes, but with caveats. A subgraph used as a `Send()` worker can itself contain parallel edges or even its own `Send()` fan-outs. This creates **hierarchical map-reduce**. However, debugging nested parallelism is significantly harder (traces show deeply nested namespaces), and memory usage compounds (each outer branch × each inner branch). Limit to 2 levels maximum. If you need more, flatten the hierarchy: pre-compute the full list of work items and do a single-level `Send()` fan-out.

---

## ✅ 1.9.17 「 Production Readiness Checklist 」

- [ ] Every state key written by parallel branches has a reducer defined
- [ ] Workers handle their own exceptions (return error status, don't raise)
- [ ] Maximum parallel branches capped (≤ 20 for `Send()`, or batched)
- [ ] Workers have timeouts for external calls (API, DB, vector store)
- [ ] Reduce node handles partial results (some successes + some errors)
- [ ] Reducers tested independently with edge cases (empty lists, None values)
- [ ] Memory usage profiled at expected max branch count
- [ ] Rate limits considered for external APIs called by parallel workers
- [ ] Integration test verifies all branches actually run in parallel (timing check)
- [ ] Monitoring tracks per-worker duration to identify slow branches
- [ ] Fallback exists for when parallel execution fails (sequential degradation)

---

## 🟡 1.9.18 「 Troubleshooting Guide 」

| Symptom | Check | Fix |
|---------|-------|-----|
| `INVALID_CONCURRENT_GRAPH_UPDATE` | Parallel branches writing to same non-reducer key | Add reducer via `Annotated[Type, reducer_fn]` |
| Only one branch result in output | Non-reducer key — last write wins silently | Switch to list with `concat` reducer |
| `Send()` creates 0 branches | Empty input list to `Send()` generator | Add guard: `if not items: return [Send("Default", ...)]` |
| Memory spike during fan-out | Too many parallel branches | Cap at 10-20, batch remainder |
| Slower than sequential | Workers are CPU-bound (GIL) | Use `ProcessPoolExecutor` inside workers, or reduce branch count |
| Reducer produces wrong result | Custom reducer has bug | Unit test reducer: `assert my_reducer([1], [2]) == [1, 2]` |
| Partial results missing | Worker crashed without error handling | Add try/except to every worker, return error status |
| Rate limit errors in workers | Too many parallel API calls | Add rate limiter or reduce branch count |
| Non-deterministic output order | List reducer appends in completion order | Sort results in reduce node if order matters |
| Timeout on reduce node | One worker is hung | Add per-worker timeout, use `concurrent.futures` |

---

## 💰 1.9.19 「 Cost Calculator 」

| Scale | Pattern | Parallel Branches | Wall-Clock Savings | Compute Cost | Infrastructure |
|-------|---------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------|----------------|
| **1K req/day** | Static (3 sources) | 1.3 | ~60% reduction | Same total CPU | No extra infra |
| **10K req/day** | Static (3 sources) | 1.3 | ~60% reduction | Same total CPU | No extra infra |
| **10K req/day** | Dynamic (5 assets/req) | 1.5 | ~80% reduction | +~20% memory | No extra infra |
| **100K req/day** | Static (3 sources) | 1.3 | ~60% reduction | Same total CPU | No extra infra |
| **100K req/day** | Dynamic (10 docs/req) | 1.10 | ~90% reduction | +~50% memory | Monitor thread pool |

| Cost Component | Sequential | Map-Reduce (3 branches) | Savings |
|----------------|-----------|------------------------|---------|
| **LLM API cost** | Same | Same | $0 (same total tokens) |
| **Compute (CPU)** | Baseline | Same (work is the same) | $0 |
| **Memory** | Baseline | +30-50% | Slight increase |
| **Wall-clock (user latency)** | 3× worker time | 1× worker time | 66% reduction |
| **User satisfaction** | Slow responses | Fast responses | Priceless |

**Key insight:** Map-reduce doesn't reduce total compute cost (the same work runs either way), but it dramatically reduces **user-perceived latency**. At 100K requests/day, cutting response time from 3s to 1s by parallelizing 3 data sources means users collectively save ~55 hours of waiting time per day. The only cost increase is memory (~50% more per concurrent request), which at typical cloud pricing is $50-100/month — trivial compared to the UX improvement.

---

## ⚪ 1.9.20 「 Quick Reference Card 」

```
╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║            MAP-REDUCE IN LANGGRAPH — QUICK REFERENCE          ║
╠════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║                                                                ║
║  STATIC FAN-OUT (fixed branches):                              ║
║    def route(state) -> List[str]:                              ║
║        return ["NodeA", "NodeB", "NodeC"]                      ║
║    graph.add_conditional_edges("Gate", route)                  ║
║                                                                ║
║  DYNAMIC FAN-OUT (runtime branches):                           ║
║    from langgraph.graph import Send                            ║
║    def route(state) -> List[Send]:                             ║
║        return [Send("Worker", {"item": x}) for x in items]    ║
║    graph.add_conditional_edges(START, route)                   ║
║                                                                ║
║  REDUCERS (required for parallel writes):                      ║
║    results: Annotated[List[str], operator.add]    # concat     ║
║    count:   Annotated[int, operator.add]          # sum        ║
║    context: Annotated[Dict, merge_dicts]          # custom     ║
║    messages: Annotated[List, add_messages]         # built-in  ║
║                                                                ║
║  WORKER PATTERN:                                               ║
║    def worker(state) -> Dict:                                  ║
║        try:                                                    ║
║            result = do_work(state["item"])                     ║
║            return {"results": [result], "errors": 0}           ║
║        except Exception as e:                                  ║
║            return {"results": [{"error": str(e)}], "errors": 1}║
║                                                                ║
║  REDUCE PATTERN:                                               ║
║    def reduce(state) -> Dict:                                  ║
║        ok = [r for r in state["results"] if "error" not in r]  ║
║        return {"final": aggregate(ok)}                         ║
║                                                                ║
║  LIMITS:                                                       ║
║    Max parallel branches: 10-20 (batch beyond)                 ║
║    Memory: ~0.5MB per branch (state copy)                      ║
║    Overhead: ~2ms per branch                                   ║
║    Best for: I/O-bound work (API, DB, vector search)           ║
║    Worst for: CPU-bound with GIL (use multiprocessing)         ║
╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
```

## ⚪ 1.9.22 「 Dependency Matrix 」

| Relationship | Topic | Why It Matters |
|-------------|-------|----------------|
| **Requires** | Chapter 1.8 (Subgraph Composition) | Map workers can be subgraphs for complex per-branch logic |
| **Requires** | Chapter 1.5 (Streaming) | Streaming from parallel branches needs `subgraphs=True` |
| **Enhances** | Chapter 1.6 (Error Recovery) | Each map worker needs independent error handling |
| **Enhances** | Chapter 1.7 (Observability) | Tracing parallel branches requires namespace-aware logging |
| **Enhances** | Chapter 1.4 (Multi-Agent) | Multi-agent patterns often use Send() for parallel agent execution |
| **Related** | Chapter 1.3 (Dynamic Graphs) | Send() is a form of dynamic graph construction at runtime |
| **Enables** | Chapter 1.10 (Alternatives) | Other frameworks handle map-reduce differently — comparison point |
| **Related** | Chapter 2.2 (Semantic Caching) | Cache checks can be a parallel branch in context gathering |
| **Related** | Chapter 2.7 (Batching) | Batching is the complement — when you CAN'T parallelize individual calls |
| **Related** | Chapter 2.3 (Context Window) | Parallel context gathering may retrieve more than the window can hold |

## 🔢 1.9.23 「 Real-World Scale Numbers 」

### At 10K Requests/Day

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| **Parallel branches per request** | 3 (vector + SQL + cache) |
| **Sequential latency** | ~3.0s per request |
| **Parallel latency** | ~1.2s per request (slowest branch) |
| **Daily time saved** | 10,000 × 1.8s = 5 hours of user wait time |
| **Memory overhead** | +~1.5MB per concurrent request (3 branches × 0.5MB) |
| **Peak concurrent branches** | ~21 (7 concurrent requests × 3 branches) |
| **Thread pool utilization** | ~20% (well within default pool) |
| **Error rate impact** | Each branch independent — 1 failure affects 1/3 of context, not all |

### At 100K Requests/Day

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| **Parallel branches per request** | 3 static + ~5 dynamic (per-asset Send()) |
| **Sequential latency** | ~8.0s (3 sources + 5 assets) |
| **Parallel latency** | ~1.5s (two-level: sources parallel + assets parallel) |
| **Daily time saved** | 100,000 × 6.5s = ~180 hours of user wait time |
| **Memory overhead** | +~4MB per concurrent request (8 branches × 0.5MB) |
| **Peak concurrent branches** | ~560 (70 concurrent requests × 8 branches) |
| **Thread pool sizing** | Need custom pool: `max_workers=100` minimum |
| **Reducer merge time** | ~5ms per request (8-branch merge) |

- **Key insight:** At 100K scale, the two-level map-reduce (parallel sources × parallel assets) turns an 8-second sequential pipeline into a 1.5-second parallel one — a 5.3× speedup. The critical scaling factor is thread pool sizing: the default Python thread pool (often 32 workers) bottlenecks at ~4 concurrent 8-branch fan-outs. Production deployments should size the thread pool to at least `peak_concurrent_requests × branches_per_request × 0.5` to avoid queuing delays. The memory cost (~4MB per concurrent request) is modest — at 70 concurrent requests, that's ~280MB, well within a single container.

---

---


# ⚡「 Chapter 1.10 」— LangGraph vs. Alternatives Comparison

> <span style="color:#00D4FF">**Advanced LangGraph**</span> · Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐ · Read Time: ~25 min

---

## 🔵 1.10.1 「 Also Known As 」

- **Framework Comparison Pattern**
- **Orchestration Layer Selection**
- **Agent Framework Decision Matrix**
- **Build vs. Buy vs. Framework Evaluation**
- **Agentic Architecture Showdown**

In the rapidly expanding ecosystem of LLM orchestration frameworks, choosing the right foundation determines everything from development velocity to production reliability. This chapter provides a rigorous, evidence-based comparison of LangGraph against its primary alternatives — LangChain Agents, CrewAI, AutoGen, Semantic Kernel, and custom orchestrators — across the dimensions that matter in production.

---

## 🔵 1.10.2 「 The Layman Explanation 」

Imagine you're building a factory. You need to decide:

1. **Build everything yourself** (custom orchestration) — total control, maximum effort
2. **Buy a turnkey factory** (high-level frameworks like CrewAI) — fast start, limited customization
3. **Buy a modular system** (LangGraph) — assemble from well-designed parts, customize each module

**LangChain Agents** are like hiring a general contractor who follows a script — great for simple jobs, but when the script breaks, debugging is a nightmare because the contractor makes opaque decisions.

**LangGraph** is like having a detailed blueprint where every decision point is explicit, every pathway is drawn, and you can see (and change) exactly what happens at each step. The trade-off is you must draw the blueprint yourself.

**CrewAI** is like hiring a team of specialized workers with predefined roles — excellent for "throw agents at this problem" scenarios, but the workers communicate through a rigid hierarchy you can't easily change.

**AutoGen** is like setting up a conference room where AI agents talk to each other — powerful for multi-agent conversation but hard to control when the conversation goes off the rails.

The key insight: **there is no universally "best" framework**. The right choice depends on your specific requirements for control, debuggability, state management, and production operation.

---

## 🔵 1.10.3 「 How It Works — Step by Step 」

### Framework Comparison Decision Process

```
╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║              FRAMEWORK EVALUATION PIPELINE                    ║
╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║                                                               ║
║  ┌─────────────────┐                                         ║

║  │     Analysis     │                                         ║
║  └────────┬────────┘                                         ║
║           │                                                   ║
║           ▼                                                   ║
║  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐                 ║

║  │  ┌─────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ │                 ║

║  │  │ Chain   │ │ Workflow │ │  Agent   │ │                 ║
║  │  └────┬────┘ └────┬─────┘ └────┬─────┘ │                 ║
║  └───────┼───────────┼────────────┼────────┘                 ║
║          │           │            │                           ║
║          ▼           ▼            ▼                           ║
║  ┌──────────┐ ┌───────────┐ ┌───────────┐                   ║
║  │LangChain │ │ LangGraph │ │ CrewAI /  │                   ║
║  │  Agent   │ │           │ │ AutoGen   │                   ║
║  └──────────┘ └───────────┘ └───────────┘                   ║
║                                                               ║
║  3. Evaluate Against Production Criteria:                     ║
║  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐                 ║
║  │  ☐ State management granularity         │                 ║
║  │  ☐ Debuggability / observability        │                 ║
║  │  ☐ Human-in-the-loop support            │                 ║
║  │  ☐ Streaming capability                 │                 ║
║  │  ☐ Persistence / checkpointing          │                 ║

║  │  ☐ Testing surface area                 │                 ║
║  │  ☐ Vendor lock-in risk                  │                 ║

║                                                               ║
║  4. Prototype & Benchmark (2-week spike)                     ║
║  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐                 ║
║  │  Build identical pipeline in top 2      │                 ║
║  │  candidates. Measure:                   │                 ║
║  │  • Lines of code                        │                 ║
║  │  • Latency (p50/p95/p99)               │                 ║
║  │  • Error scenarios handled              │                 ║
║  │  • Time to add new feature              │                 ║
║  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘                 ║
║                                                               ║
```
