# 📊 Part 1 — Comparison Matrix: Advanced LangGraph (Chapters 1.1–1.10)

> This matrix provides a cross-chapter comparison of all Part 1 patterns, helping you choose the right approach for your specific use case.

---

## Master Comparison Table

| Dimension | Ch1: HITL | Ch2: Persistence | Ch3: Dynamic Graphs | Ch4: Multi-Agent | Ch5: Streaming | Ch6: Error Recovery | Ch7: Observability | Ch8: Subgraphs | Ch9: Map-Reduce | Ch10: Alternatives |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|---------------------|------------------|----------------|--------------------|--------------------|----------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Complexity** | Medium | Medium | High | High | Medium-High | Medium | Low-Medium | High | High | N/A (meta) |
| **LLM Calls** | 0-1 extra | 0 | 0-N | N agents | 0 | 0-3 retries | 0 | Varies | N parallel | Varies |
| **Latency Impact** | +500ms-5min (human) | +15ms (checkpoint) | +50ms (build) | +2-10× | -50% (TTFT) | +0-1.5s (retries) | +5ms (logging) | +15ms (compile) | ÷N (parallel) | Framework-dependent |
| **State Impact** | Adds approval fields | Serialization cost | Dynamic schema | Per-agent state | Stream buffers | Retry counters | Log volume | Isolated substates | Reducer merges | State format varies |
| **Production Must-Have** | Depends | Yes | Rarely | Sometimes | Usually | Always | Always | Often | Sometimes | Once (decision) |
| **DC-Copilot Uses** | No | Yes (DynamoDB) | No | Yes (2 subgraphs) | Yes (SSE) | Yes (per-node) | Yes (structured) | Yes (2 compiled) | Yes (fan-out) | Yes (chose LangGraph) |

---

## Pattern Selection Guide

### By Use Case

| Use Case | Primary Pattern | Supporting Patterns |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|
| Multi-turn chatbot | Ch2 (Persistence) | Ch5 (Streaming), Ch6 (Error Recovery) |
| Content moderation pipeline | Ch1 (HITL) | Ch8 (Subgraphs), Ch7 (Observability) |
| Document processing at scale | Ch9 (Map-Reduce) | Ch6 (Error Recovery), Ch3 (Dynamic) |
| Multi-agent collaboration | Ch4 (Multi-Agent) | Ch8 (Subgraphs), Ch9 (Map-Reduce) |
| Real-time streaming app | Ch5 (Streaming) | Ch6 (Error Recovery), Ch2 (Persistence) |
| Intent-based routing system | Ch3 (Dynamic) | Ch8 (Subgraphs), Ch6 (Error Recovery) |
| Production deployment | Ch6 + Ch7 | Ch2 (Persistence), Ch5 (Streaming) |
| Framework migration | Ch10 (Alternatives) | All patterns for evaluation |

### By Team Maturity

| Team Level | Start With | Then Add | Advanced |
|------------|-----------|----------|----------|
| **Beginner** | Ch2 (Persistence), Ch5 (Streaming) | Ch6 (Error Recovery), Ch7 (Observability) | Ch1 (HITL), Ch3 (Dynamic) |
| **Intermediate** | Ch6 + Ch7 (Production basics) | Ch8 (Subgraphs), Ch9 (Map-Reduce) | Ch4 (Multi-Agent), Ch3 (Dynamic) |
| **Advanced** | Ch4 (Multi-Agent), Ch3 (Dynamic) | Ch9 (Map-Reduce at scale) | Custom patterns combining all |

---

## Interaction Matrix

Shows how patterns combine and depend on each other:

```
         Ch1  Ch2  Ch3  Ch4  Ch5  Ch6  Ch7  Ch8  Ch9  Ch10
Ch1  HITL  ──   REQ  OPT  OPT  OPT  REQ  REQ  OPT  OPT   ──
Ch2  Pers  REQ  ──   OPT  OPT  OPT  OPT  OPT  OPT  OPT   ──
Ch3  Dyn   OPT  OPT  ──   USE  OPT  REQ  OPT  USE  OPT   ──
Ch4  Multi OPT  OPT  USE  ──   USE  REQ  REQ  REQ  USE   ──
Ch5  Strm  OPT  OPT  OPT  USE  ──   OPT  OPT  USE  OPT   ──
Ch6  Err   REQ  OPT  REQ  REQ  OPT  ──   REQ  OPT  OPT   ──
Ch7  Obs   REQ  OPT  OPT  REQ  OPT  REQ  ──   OPT  OPT   ──
Ch8  Sub   OPT  OPT  USE  REQ  USE  OPT  OPT  ──   USE   ──
Ch9  MR    OPT  OPT  OPT  USE  OPT  OPT  OPT  USE  ──    ──
Ch10 Alt   ──   ──   ──   ──   ──   ──   ──   ──   ──    ──

Legend: REQ = requires, USE = commonly used together, OPT = optional enhancement
```

---

## Cost Impact Summary (at 10K requests/day)

| Pattern | Monthly Infrastructure | Monthly LLM Overhead | Development Investment |
|---------|----------------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Ch1: HITL | +$0 | +$0 (human replaces LLM call) | 2-3 weeks |
| Ch2: Persistence | +$50 (DynamoDB) | +$0 | 1-2 weeks |
| Ch3: Dynamic Graphs | +$0 | +$0-300 (dynamic routing) | 2-4 weeks |
| Ch4: Multi-Agent | +$0 | +$1,500-6,000 (N agents) | 3-6 weeks |
| Ch5: Streaming | +$0 | +$0 | 1-2 weeks |
| Ch6: Error Recovery | +$0 | +$300-900 (retries) | 1-2 weeks |
| Ch7: Observability | +$200-400 (LangSmith) | +$0 | 1 week |
| Ch8: Subgraphs | +$0 | +$0 | 2-3 weeks |
| Ch9: Map-Reduce | +$0 | +$0-300 (parallel) | 2-3 weeks |
| Ch10: Alternatives | +$0 (evaluation only) | +$200-2,000 (POC) | 2 weeks (POC) |

---

# 🏁 Part 1 Summary — Advanced LangGraph

## What We Covered

Part 1 explored **10 advanced LangGraph patterns** that transform basic graph workflows into production-grade systems:

1. **Human-in-the-Loop** (Ch1) — `interrupt_before/after` gives humans control at any node, with state editing and approval workflows.

2. **Persistence & Time-Travel** (Ch2) — Checkpointers (DynamoDB, SQLite, PostgreSQL) persist state between turns, enabling multi-turn conversations and failure recovery.

3. **Dynamic Graph Construction** (Ch3) — Build graphs at runtime based on configuration, user context, or external signals. Treat graphs as data, not code.

4. **Multi-Agent Orchestration** (Ch4) — Compose specialized agents as subgraph nodes with supervisor routing, shared state, and parallel execution.

5. **Streaming & Async** (Ch5) — Token-level streaming through subgraphs via `stream_mode="custom"` and `get_stream_writer()`, with SSE delivery to clients.

6. **Error Recovery & Retry** (Ch6) — Per-node retry policies, circuit breakers, safe defaults, and graceful degradation keep pipelines running when components fail.

7. **Observability** (Ch7) — LangSmith tracing, structured logging, and custom metrics provide visibility into every node transition and state mutation.

8. **Subgraph Composition** (Ch8) — Compiled subgraphs as nodes enable modular, testable, reusable graph components with state isolation.

9. **Map-Reduce Patterns** (Ch9) — Static fan-out, `Send()` dynamic dispatch, and custom reducers enable parallel processing with typed state merge.

10. **Framework Comparison** (Ch10) — LangGraph vs. LangChain Agents vs. CrewAI vs. AutoGen vs. Custom: weighted evaluation, POC methodology, and ADR documentation.

## The DC-Copilot Thread

Throughout Part 1, we traced these patterns through the DC-Copilot codebase:

- **Architecture:** Parent graph → LanguageTranslation → ProfanityCheck → parallel(CopilotFlow, SummarizationSubgraph) → END
- **Persistence:** DynamoDB checkpointer for multi-turn conversations
- **Streaming:** `stream_mode="custom"` with `subgraphs=True` for SSE delivery
- **Error Recovery:** Per-node safe defaults (ProfanityNode, ClassifyIntentNode, IntentFanoutNode, ContextGatheringNode)
- **Subgraphs:** CopilotFlow and SummarizationSubgraph as compiled subgraphs with isolated state
- **Map-Reduce:** Parallel fan-out to both subgraphs, per-asset context pre-computation
- **Framework Choice:** LangGraph chosen over LangChain Agents (non-deterministic), CrewAI (no persistence), and Custom (8 weeks vs. 2)

## Key Takeaways

1. **Explicitness wins in production.** Every pattern in Part 1 favors explicit control over implicit magic — typed state over opaque dicts, explicit edges over LLM-driven routing, visible checkpoints over hidden memory.

2. **Patterns compose.** The real power emerges when combining patterns: subgraphs + map-reduce for parallel processing, persistence + error recovery for reliability, streaming + HITL for interactive workflows.

3. **Start simple, add patterns as needed.** Begin with persistence (Ch2) and error recovery (Ch6). Add streaming (Ch5) when users need real-time feedback. Add subgraphs (Ch8) when the graph exceeds ~10 nodes. Add HITL (Ch1) when human oversight is required.

4. **The framework is not the product.** LangGraph is infrastructure — the value comes from the domain logic inside each node. Part 2 focuses on the other critical dimension: making LLM calls as efficient and cost-effective as possible.

---

> **Next:** Part 2 dives into **Token & Prompt Reduction** — 10 chapters on compressing prompts, caching responses, managing context windows, routing between models, and building cost dashboards. If Part 1 was about _orchestrating_ LLM calls correctly, Part 2 is about _making each call count_.

---

---

# 💰 Part 2 — Advanced Token & Prompt Reduction

> _"The cheapest token is the one you never send."_

---

---


# 💰「 Chapter 2.1 」— Prompt Compression Libraries

> <span style="color:#00D4FF">**Token & Prompt Reduction**</span> · Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐ · Read Time: ~25 min

---

## 🔵 2.1.1 「 Also Known As 」

- **Prompt Distillation**
- **Context Compression**
- **Token-Efficient Prompting**
- **Selective Context Extraction**
- **LLM-Lingua Pattern**

Prompt compression reduces token count while preserving semantic meaning, cutting costs and improving latency without degrading response quality. This chapter covers the major libraries, algorithms, and production patterns for compressing prompts before they reach the LLM.

---

## 🔵 2.1.2 「 The Layman Explanation 」

Imagine you're writing a letter to someone who charges by the word. You have a 10-page report you want them to read and respond to. You have three options:

1. **Send the whole thing** — expensive, slow for them to read
2. **Manually summarize** — you might miss important details
3. **Use a smart highlighter** — automatically marks only the sentences that matter for your question, removes filler

Prompt compression is option 3. Libraries like LLMLingua, LongLLMLingua, and selective context analyze your prompt, identify which tokens carry actual information for the task at hand, and remove the rest. A 10,000-token prompt might compress to 3,000 tokens with <5% quality loss — saving 70% on cost and ~40% on latency.

The key trade-off: compression takes compute time (typically 50-200ms), so it only pays off when the saved tokens exceed the compression overhead — usually at prompts above 2,000 tokens.

---

## 🔵 2.1.3 「 How It Works — Step by Step 」

### Prompt Compression Pipeline

```
╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║              PROMPT COMPRESSION PIPELINE                      ║
╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║                                                               ║
║  ┌──────────────────┐                                        ║
║  │  Original Prompt  │  (10,000 tokens)                      ║
║  │  ┌──────────────┐│                                        ║
║  │  │ System Msg   ││                                        ║
║  │  │ Context Docs ││                                        ║
║  │  │ User Query   ││                                        ║
║  │  └──────────────┘│                                        ║
║  └────────┬─────────┘                                        ║
║           │                                                   ║
║           ▼                                                   ║
║  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐                    ║
║  │  1. TOKENIZE & SEGMENT               │                    ║
║  │  Split into: instruction │ context │  │                    ║
║  │  demonstration │ question             │                    ║
║  └────────┬─────────────────────────────┘                    ║
║           │                                                   ║
║           ▼                                                   ║
║  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐                    ║
║  │  2. COMPUTE TOKEN IMPORTANCE          │                    ║
║  │  ┌────────────────────────────────┐  │                    ║
║  │  │ Method A: Perplexity-based     │  │                    ║
║  │  │  Small LM scores each token    │  │                    ║
║  │  │  High perplexity = important   │  │                    ║
║  │  ├────────────────────────────────┤  │                    ║
║  │  │ Method B: Entropy-based        │  │                    ║
║  │  │  Information entropy per token │  │                    ║
║  │  │  High entropy = keep           │  │                    ║
║  │  ├────────────────────────────────┤  │                    ║
║  │  │ Method C: Query-aware          │  │                    ║
║  │  │  Relevance to user question    │  │                    ║
║  │  │  Score tokens by query match   │  │                    ║
║  │  └────────────────────────────────┘  │                    ║
║  └────────┬─────────────────────────────┘                    ║
║           │                                                   ║
║           ▼                                                   ║
║  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐                    ║
║  │  3. FILTER BY THRESHOLD               │                    ║
║  │  Keep tokens above importance cutoff  │                    ║
║  │  Target: compression_ratio (e.g. 0.3) │                    ║
║  └────────┬─────────────────────────────┘                    ║
║           │                                                   ║
║           ▼                                                   ║
║  ┌──────────────────┐                                        ║
║  │ Compressed Prompt │  (3,000 tokens = 70% reduction)       ║
║  └──────────────────┘                                        ║
║                                                               ║
║  Savings: ~$0.07 per call (GPT-4) × 10K calls = $700/day    ║
╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
```

### Compression Algorithm Comparison

```
╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║  ALGORITHM ARCHITECTURES                                      ║
╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║                                                               ║
║  LLMLingua (Perplexity-based):                               ║
║  ┌─────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐  ║
║  │ Prompt  │──▶│ Small LM │──▶│Perplexity│──▶│  Filter  │  ║
║  │ Tokens  │   │ (GPT-2)  │   │ Scores   │   │ by Ratio │  ║
║  └─────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘  ║
║                                                               ║
║  LongLLMLingua (Query-aware):                                ║
║  ┌─────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐  ║
║  │ Prompt  │──▶│ Small LM │──▶│ Query-   │──▶│ Coarse→  │  ║
║  │ + Query │   │ (Llama)  │   │ Aware    │   │   Fine   │  ║
║  └─────────┘   └──────────┘   │ Scoring  │   │ Filter   │  ║
║                               └──────────┘   └──────────┘  ║
║                                                               ║
║  Selective Context (Entropy-based):                           ║
║  ┌─────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐  ║
║  │ Prompt  │──▶│Self-Info │──▶│ Entropy  │──▶│  Lexical │  ║
║  │ Sents   │   │ Scoring  │   │ Ranking  │   │  Filter  │  ║
║  └─────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘  ║
╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
```

---

## 🟢 2.1.4 「 Example 1: LLMLingua Prompt Compression 」

<details>
<summary>📝 Click to expand full production code</summary>

```python
"""
Production prompt compression using LLMLingua.
Demonstrates perplexity-based token filtering with budget control.
"""

from llmlingua import PromptCompressor
from typing import Optional
import time
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class ProductionPromptCompressor:
    """
    Wraps LLMLingua for production use with caching,
    metrics, and fallback to uncompressed prompt.
    """
    
    def __init__(
        self,
        model_name: str = "microsoft/llmlingua-2-bert-base-multilingual-cased-meetingbank",
        use_llmlingua2: bool = True,
        device: str = "cpu",
    ):
        self.compressor = PromptCompressor(
            model_name=model_name,
            use_llmlingua2=use_llmlingua2,
            device_map=device,
        )
        self._compression_count = 0
        self._total_tokens_saved = 0
    
    def compress(
        self,
        prompt: str,
        target_ratio: float = 0.3,
        instruction: str = "",
        question: str = "",
        min_tokens_to_compress: int = 500,
        force_context_ids: Optional[list[int]] = None,
    ) -> dict:
        """
        Compress a prompt with production safeguards.
        
        Args:
            prompt: The context/document portion to compress
            target_ratio: Target compression ratio (0.3 = keep 30%)
            instruction: System instruction (never compressed)
            question: User question (never compressed)
            min_tokens_to_compress: Skip compression below this threshold
            force_context_ids: Indices of context segments to always keep
            
        Returns:
            Dict with compressed_prompt, metrics, and original_tokens
        """
        original_tokens = self._estimate_tokens(prompt)
        
        if original_tokens < min_tokens_to_compress:
            logger.info(
                f"Skipping compression: {original_tokens} tokens "
                f"below threshold {min_tokens_to_compress}"
            )
            return {
                "compressed_prompt": self._build_full_prompt(instruction, prompt, question),
                "original_tokens": original_tokens,
                "compressed_tokens": original_tokens,
                "ratio": 1.0,
                "compression_time_ms": 0,
                "was_compressed": False,
            }
        
        start = time.perf_counter()
        
        try:
            result = self.compressor.compress_prompt(
                context=[prompt],
                instruction=instruction,
                question=question,
                rate=target_ratio,
                force_context_ids=force_context_ids or [],
                condition_in_question="after_condition",
                reorder_context="sort",
                dynamic_context_compression_ratio=0.3,
                condition_compare=True,
                context_budget="+100",
                token_budget_ratio=1.4,
            )
            
            elapsed_ms = (time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000
            compressed_tokens = self._estimate_tokens(result["compressed_prompt"])
            tokens_saved = original_tokens - compressed_tokens
            actual_ratio = compressed_tokens / original_tokens
            
            self._compression_count += 1
            self._total_tokens_saved += tokens_saved
            
            logger.info(
                f"Compressed {original_tokens} → {compressed_tokens} tokens "
                f"(ratio={actual_ratio:.2f}, saved={tokens_saved}, "
                f"time={elapsed_ms:.0f}ms)"
            )
            
            return {
                "compressed_prompt": result["compressed_prompt"],
                "original_tokens": original_tokens,
                "compressed_tokens": compressed_tokens,
                "ratio": actual_ratio,
                "compression_time_ms": elapsed_ms,
                "was_compressed": True,
                "origin_tokens": result.get("origin_tokens", original_tokens),
            }
            
        except Exception as e:
            elapsed_ms = (time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000
            logger.error(f"Compression failed ({elapsed_ms:.0f}ms): {e}", exc_info=True)
            
            return {
                "compressed_prompt": self._build_full_prompt(instruction, prompt, question),
                "original_tokens": original_tokens,
                "compressed_tokens": original_tokens,
                "ratio": 1.0,
                "compression_time_ms": elapsed_ms,
                "was_compressed": False,
                "error": str(e),
            }
    
    def _build_full_prompt(self, instruction: str, context: str, question: str) -> str:
        parts = []
        if instruction:
            parts.append(instruction)
        parts.append(context)
        if question:
            parts.append(question)
        return "\n\n".join(parts)
    
    def _estimate_tokens(self, text: str) -> int:
        return len(text) // 4
    
    @property
    def stats(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "total_compressions": self._compression_count,
            "total_tokens_saved": self._total_tokens_saved,
            "avg_tokens_saved": (
                self._total_tokens_saved / self._compression_count
                if self._compression_count > 0 else 0
            ),
        }


# ============================================================
# Usage example
# ============================================================

def demo_compression():
    compressor = ProductionPromptCompressor()
    
    instruction = "You are a maintenance assistant. Answer based on the provided context."
    
    context = """
    Work Order WO-2024-4521: Pump P-301 maintenance report.
    Date: 2024-03-15. Asset: Centrifugal pump, Model XR-500.
    
    The pump was inspected on March 10, 2024. During the inspection,
    the following issues were identified:
    1. Bearing temperature elevated to 85°C (normal range: 40-65°C)
    2. Vibration levels at 12mm/s (acceptable: <7mm/s)
    3. Seal leak detected at discharge side, approximately 2 drops/minute
    4. Motor current draw at 95% of rated capacity
    
    Previous maintenance history:
    - 2023-12-01: Bearing replacement (scheduled)
    - 2023-09-15: Seal replacement (unscheduled, leak detected)
    - 2023-06-01: Full overhaul including impeller balancing
    - 2023-03-01: Vibration analysis, within limits
    - 2022-12-01: Bearing replacement (scheduled)
    
    The pump operates 24/7 in a corrosive environment (pH 3.5 process fluid).
    Expected bearing life in this environment is 6-8 months versus the
    standard 12-month replacement interval. The seal material was upgraded
    from Viton to PTFE in the September 2023 replacement.
    
    Recommended actions:
    1. Replace bearings immediately (3-month old, showing signs of premature wear)
    2. Monitor seal leak - schedule replacement if rate exceeds 5 drops/minute
    3. Check motor alignment (high current draw may indicate misalignment)
    4. Consider upgrading to ceramic bearings for corrosive environment
    
    Additional notes from field technician:
    The pump foundation shows signs of settling on the north side,
    approximately 2mm deviation from level. This may be contributing
    to the elevated vibration and bearing wear. Foundation leveling
    should be scheduled as part of the next planned outage.
    """ * 3  # Repeat to simulate large context
    
    question = "What is the root cause of the elevated bearing temperature?"
    
    result = compressor.compress(
        prompt=context,
        instruction=instruction,
        question=question,
        target_ratio=0.4,
    )
    
    print(f"Original tokens:    {result['original_tokens']}")
    print(f"Compressed tokens:  {result['compressed_tokens']}")
    print(f"Compression ratio:  {result['ratio']:.2f}")
    print(f"Compression time:   {result['compression_time_ms']:.0f}ms")
    print(f"Was compressed:     {result['was_compressed']}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    demo_compression()
```

</details>

---

## 🟢 2.1.5 「 Example 2: Selective Context with Entropy Scoring 」

<details>
<summary>📝 Click to expand full production code</summary>

```python
"""
Entropy-based selective context compression.
Removes low-information sentences while preserving critical content.
"""

import math
import re
from typing import Optional
from collections import Counter
from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class ScoredSentence:
    text: str
    index: int
    entropy: float
    word_count: int
    is_critical: bool = False


class SelectiveContextCompressor:
    """
    Entropy-based sentence selection for prompt compression.
    
    Keeps sentences with high information entropy (surprising/novel content)
    and removes filler (repetitive, low-entropy sentences).
    """
    
    def __init__(
        self,
        critical_patterns: Optional[list[str]] = None,
        min_entropy_percentile: float = 0.4,
    ):
        self.critical_patterns = critical_patterns or [
            r'\b\d+\s*°[CF]\b',          # Temperature readings
            r'\b\d+\s*mm/s\b',            # Vibration readings
            r'\berror\b|\bfault\b',       # Error indicators
            r'\breplac\w+\b',             # Replacement mentions
            r'\bfail\w+\b',              # Failure mentions
            r'\bwarning\b|\balarm\b',     # Alert indicators
        ]
        self.min_entropy_percentile = min_entropy_percentile
    
    def compress(
        self,
        context: str,
        target_ratio: float = 0.5,
        preserve_first_n: int = 2,
        preserve_last_n: int = 1,
    ) -> dict:
        """
        Compress context by removing low-entropy sentences.
        
        Args:
            context: Text to compress
            target_ratio: Fraction of sentences to keep (0.5 = keep 50%)
            preserve_first_n: Always keep first N sentences
            preserve_last_n: Always keep last N sentences
            
        Returns:
            Dict with compressed text and metrics
        """
        sentences = self._split_sentences(context)
        if len(sentences) <= preserve_first_n + preserve_last_n:
            return {
                "compressed": context,
                "original_sentences": len(sentences),
                "kept_sentences": len(sentences),
                "ratio": 1.0,
            }
        
        scored = self._score_sentences(sentences)
        
        target_count = max(
            int(len(scored) * target_ratio),
            preserve_first_n + preserve_last_n
        )
        
        for s in scored[:preserve_first_n]:
            s.is_critical = True
        for s in scored[-preserve_last_n:]:
            s.is_critical = True
        
        for s in scored:
            if not s.is_critical:
                for pattern in self.critical_patterns:
                    if re.search(pattern, s.text, re.IGNORECASE):
                        s.is_critical = True
                        break
        
        critical_count = sum(1 for s in scored if s.is_critical)
        remaining_slots = max(target_count - critical_count, 0)
        
        non_critical = [s for s in scored if not s.is_critical]
        non_critical.sort(key=lambda s: s.entropy, reverse=True)
        
        for s in non_critical[:remaining_slots]:
            s.is_critical = True
        
        kept = sorted(
            [s for s in scored if s.is_critical],
            key=lambda s: s.index
        )
        
        compressed = " ".join(s.text for s in kept)
        
        original_words = sum(s.word_count for s in scored)
        kept_words = sum(s.word_count for s in kept)
        
        return {
            "compressed": compressed,
            "original_sentences": len(scored),
            "kept_sentences": len(kept),
            "original_words": original_words,
            "kept_words": kept_words,
            "ratio": kept_words / original_words if original_words > 0 else 1.0,
            "avg_entropy_kept": (
                sum(s.entropy for s in kept) / len(kept) if kept else 0
            ),
            "avg_entropy_removed": (
                sum(s.entropy for s in scored if not s.is_critical)
                / max(sum(1 for s in scored if not s.is_critical), 1)
            ),
        }
    
    def _split_sentences(self, text: str) -> list[str]:
        """Split text into sentences."""
        sentences = re.split(r'(?<=[.!?])\s+', text.strip())
        return [s.strip() for s in sentences if s.strip()]
    
    def _score_sentences(self, sentences: list[str]) -> list[ScoredSentence]:
        """Score each sentence by self-information (entropy)."""
        all_words = []
        for s in sentences:
            all_words.extend(self._tokenize(s))
        
        word_freq = Counter(all_words)
        total = len(all_words)
        
        scored = []
        for i, sentence in enumerate(sentences):
            words = self._tokenize(sentence)
            if not words:
                scored.append(ScoredSentence(
                    text=sentence, index=i, entropy=0.0, word_count=0
                ))
                continue
            
            entropy = 0.0
            for word in words:
                freq = word_freq[word] / total
                if freq > 0:
                    entropy += -math.log2(freq)
            
            avg_entropy = entropy / len(words)
            
            scored.append(ScoredSentence(
                text=sentence,
                index=i,
                entropy=avg_entropy,
                word_count=len(words),
            ))
        
        return scored
    
    def _tokenize(self, text: str) -> list[str]:
        """Simple word tokenization."""
        return re.findall(r'\b\w+\b', text.lower())


# ============================================================
# Usage
# ============================================================

def demo_selective_context():
    compressor = SelectiveContextCompressor(
        critical_patterns=[
            r'\b\d+\s*°[CF]\b',
            r'\b\d+\s*mm/s\b',
            r'\bfail\w*\b',
            r'\breplac\w*\b',
            r'\berror\b',
        ]
    )
    
    context = """
    Work Order WO-2024-4521 was created on March 15, 2024.
    The work order is assigned to Team Alpha for execution.
    Pump P-301 is a centrifugal pump manufactured by AcmePumps.
    The pump model is XR-500 with a rated capacity of 500 GPM.
    Bearing temperature was measured at 85°C which exceeds the normal range of 40-65°C.
    Vibration levels recorded at 12mm/s compared to acceptable threshold of 7mm/s.
    The pump is located in Building 3, Bay 7, on the ground floor.
    Previous bearing replacement was performed on December 1, 2023.
    The facility operates three shifts, seven days per week.
    Seal failure detected at discharge side with 2 drops per minute leak rate.
    The maintenance team consists of 4 certified technicians.
    Motor current draw is at 95% of rated capacity suggesting possible misalignment.
    The parts warehouse is located in Building 1 adjacent to the workshop.
    Recommended immediate bearing replacement due to premature wear signs.
    """
    
    result = compressor.compress(context, target_ratio=0.5)
    
    print(f"Original:   {result['original_sentences']} sentences, {result['original_words']} words")
    print(f"Compressed: {result['kept_sentences']} sentences, {result['kept_words']} words")
    print(f"Ratio:      {result['ratio']:.2f}")
    print(f"\nCompressed text:\n{result['compressed']}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    demo_selective_context()
```

</details>

## 🟢 2.1.6 「 Example 3: Adaptive Compression with Quality Guard 」

<details>
<summary>📝 Click to expand full production code</summary>

```python
"""
Adaptive prompt compression that automatically adjusts compression ratio
based on quality feedback. Implements a feedback loop:
compress → invoke LLM → score quality → adjust ratio.
"""

from typing import TypedDict, Optional, Callable
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
import time
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


@dataclass
class CompressionConfig:
    initial_ratio: float = 0.5
    min_ratio: float = 0.2
    max_ratio: float = 0.9
    quality_threshold: float = 0.75
    step_size: float = 0.1
    max_iterations: int = 3


@dataclass
class CompressionResult:
    compressed_prompt: str
    original_tokens: int
    compressed_tokens: int
    final_ratio: float
    quality_score: float
    iterations: int
    total_time_ms: float
    history: list = field(default_factory=list)


class AdaptiveCompressor:
    """
    Binary-search compression: finds the maximum compression
    that maintains response quality above threshold.
    """
    
    def __init__(
        self,
        compress_fn: Callable[[str, float], str],
        quality_fn: Callable[[str, str, str], float],
        config: Optional[CompressionConfig] = None,
    ):
        self.compress_fn = compress_fn
        self.quality_fn = quality_fn
        self.config = config or CompressionConfig()
        self._ratio_history: list[tuple[float, float]] = []
    
    def compress_adaptive(
        self,
        prompt: str,
        question: str,
        reference_response: Optional[str] = None,
    ) -> CompressionResult:
        """
        Iteratively compress, checking quality at each step.
        
        Uses binary search between min_ratio and max_ratio to find
        the optimal compression level.
        """
        start = time.perf_counter()
        original_tokens = len(prompt) // 4
        
        lo = self.config.min_ratio
        hi = self.config.max_ratio
        best_result = None
        history = []
        
        for iteration in range(self.config.max_iterations):
            ratio = (lo + hi) / 2
            
            compressed = self.compress_fn(prompt, ratio)
            compressed_tokens = len(compressed) // 4
            
            quality = self.quality_fn(compressed, question, reference_response or "")
            
            history.append({
                "iteration": iteration,
                "ratio": ratio,
                "tokens": compressed_tokens,
                "quality": quality,
            })
            
            self._ratio_history.append((ratio, quality))
            
            logger.info(
                f"Iteration {iteration}: ratio={ratio:.2f}, "
                f"tokens={compressed_tokens}, quality={quality:.3f}"
            )
            
            if quality >= self.config.quality_threshold:
                best_result = CompressionResult(
                    compressed_prompt=compressed,
                    original_tokens=original_tokens,
                    compressed_tokens=compressed_tokens,
                    final_ratio=ratio,
                    quality_score=quality,
                    iterations=iteration + 1,
                    total_time_ms=0,
                    history=history,
                )
                hi = ratio
            else:
                lo = ratio
            
            if hi - lo < self.config.step_size:
                break
        
        elapsed_ms = (time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000
        
        if best_result is None:
            logger.warning("No compression met quality threshold, returning original")
            return CompressionResult(
                compressed_prompt=prompt,
                original_tokens=original_tokens,
                compressed_tokens=original_tokens,
                final_ratio=1.0,
                quality_score=1.0,
                iterations=len(history),
                total_time_ms=elapsed_ms,
                history=history,
            )
        
        best_result.total_time_ms = elapsed_ms
        return best_result
    
    @property
    def learned_optimal_ratio(self) -> float:
        """Return the average ratio that met quality threshold."""
        passing = [r for r, q in self._ratio_history if q >= self.config.quality_threshold]
        if not passing:
            return self.config.initial_ratio
        return sum(passing) / len(passing)


# ============================================================
# Quality scoring functions
# ============================================================

class QualityScorer:
    """Score response quality for compression feedback loop."""
    
    @staticmethod
    def keyword_overlap_score(
        compressed_prompt: str,
        question: str,
        reference: str,
    ) -> float:
        """Fast heuristic: keyword overlap between compressed and question."""
        q_words = set(question.lower().split())
        c_words = set(compressed_prompt.lower().split())
        
        if not q_words:
            return 1.0
        
        overlap = len(q_words & c_words) / len(q_words)
        length_penalty = min(len(c_words) / max(len(compressed_prompt.split()) * 0.3, 1), 1.0)
        
        return overlap * 0.7 + length_penalty * 0.3
    
    @staticmethod
    def rouge_l_score(
        compressed_prompt: str,
        question: str,
        reference: str,
    ) -> float:
        """ROUGE-L score between compressed and reference response."""
        if not reference:
            return QualityScorer.keyword_overlap_score(compressed_prompt, question, reference)
        
        def lcs_length(x: list[str], y: list[str]) -> int:
            m, n = len(x), len(y)
            dp = [[0] * (n + 1) for _ in range(m + 1)]
            for i in range(1, m + 1):
                for j in range(1, n + 1):
                    if x[i-1] == y[j-1]:
                        dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1
                    else:
                        dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
            return dp[m][n]
        
        ref_words = reference.lower().split()
        comp_words = compressed_prompt.lower().split()
        
        if not ref_words or not comp_words:
            return 0.0
        
        lcs = lcs_length(ref_words, comp_words)
        precision = lcs / len(comp_words)
        recall = lcs / len(ref_words)
        
        if precision + recall == 0:
            return 0.0
        
        f1 = 2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall)
        return f1


# ============================================================
# Full pipeline example
# ============================================================

def demo_adaptive_compression():
    """Demonstrate adaptive compression with quality feedback."""
    
    def simple_compress(text: str, ratio: float) -> str:
        """Simplified compression: keep ratio fraction of sentences."""
        sentences = [s.strip() for s in text.split('.') if s.strip()]
        keep_count = max(int(len(sentences) * ratio), 1)
        return '. '.join(sentences[:keep_count]) + '.'
    
    compressor = AdaptiveCompressor(
        compress_fn=simple_compress,
        quality_fn=QualityScorer.keyword_overlap_score,
        config=CompressionConfig(
            initial_ratio=0.5,
            min_ratio=0.2,
            max_ratio=0.8,
            quality_threshold=0.6,
            max_iterations=4,
        ),
    )
    
    prompt = """
    Pump P-301 bearing temperature is 85°C, above the 65°C limit.
    Vibration at 12mm/s exceeds the 7mm/s threshold.
    Last bearing replacement was December 2023.
    Seal leak detected at 2 drops per minute.
    Motor current at 95% rated capacity.
    Foundation settling of 2mm detected on north side.
    Previous overhaul performed June 2023.
    Operating in pH 3.5 corrosive environment.
    Expected bearing life is 6-8 months in this environment.
    Ceramic bearing upgrade recommended for corrosion resistance.
    """
    
    question = "What is causing the elevated bearing temperature?"
    
    result = compressor.compress_adaptive(prompt, question)
    
    print(f"Original tokens:   {result.original_tokens}")
    print(f"Compressed tokens: {result.compressed_tokens}")
    print(f"Final ratio:       {result.final_ratio:.2f}")
    print(f"Quality score:     {result.quality_score:.3f}")
    print(f"Iterations:        {result.iterations}")
    print(f"Total time:        {result.total_time_ms:.0f}ms")
    print(f"\nCompressed:\n{result.compressed_prompt}")
    
    print(f"\nLearned optimal ratio: {compressor.learned_optimal_ratio:.2f}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    demo_adaptive_compression()
```

</details>

---

## 🟢 2.1.7 「 How to Implement (Production Guide) 」

### Step 1: Measure Your Baseline

```python
import tiktoken

def measure_prompt_baseline(prompts: list[str], model: str = "gpt-4o") -> dict:
    """Measure current token usage before adding compression."""
    enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(model)
    
    token_counts = [len(enc.encode(p)) for p in prompts]
    
    return {
        "total_prompts": len(prompts),
        "avg_tokens": sum(token_counts) / len(token_counts),
        "max_tokens": max(token_counts),
        "p95_tokens": sorted(token_counts)[int(len(token_counts) * 0.95)],
        "total_tokens": sum(token_counts),
        "monthly_cost_gpt4o": sum(token_counts) * 2.50 / 1_000_000,
        "compressible_count": sum(1 for t in token_counts if t > 2000),
    }
```

### Step 2: Choose Compression Strategy

```python
def choose_compression_strategy(baseline: dict) -> str:
    """Select compression approach based on prompt profile."""
    avg = baseline["avg_tokens"]
    compressible_pct = baseline["compressible_count"] / baseline["total_prompts"]
    
    if avg < 500:
        return "none"
    
    if avg < 2000:
        return "selective_context"
    
    if compressible_pct > 0.5:
        return "llmlingua"
    
    return "adaptive"
```

### Step 3: Integrate Compression into Pipeline

```python
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, START, END
from typing import TypedDict

class CompressedState(TypedDict):
    query: str
    context: str
    compressed_context: str
    compression_ratio: float
    response: str

def build_compressed_pipeline():
    graph = StateGraph(CompressedState)
    
    def gather_context(state):
        context = retrieve_from_vector_store(state["query"])
        return {"context": context}
    
    def compress_context(state):
        compressor = get_compressor()
        result = compressor.compress(
            prompt=state["context"],
            question=state["query"],
            target_ratio=0.4,
        )
        return {
            "compressed_context": result["compressed_prompt"],
            "compression_ratio": result["ratio"],
        }
    
    def generate_response(state):
        response = llm.invoke(state["compressed_context"])
        return {"response": response}
    
    graph.add_node("gather", gather_context)
    graph.add_node("compress", compress_context)
    graph.add_node("generate", generate_response)
    
    graph.add_edge(START, "gather")
    graph.add_edge("gather", "compress")
    graph.add_edge("compress", "generate")
    graph.add_edge("generate", END)
    
    return graph.compile()
```

### Step 4: Monitor Compression Quality

```python
def monitor_compression_quality(
    original: str,
    compressed: str,
    response_original: str,
    response_compressed: str,
) -> dict:
    """Track whether compression degrades response quality."""
    from rouge_score import rouge_scorer
    
    scorer = rouge_scorer.RougeScorer(['rouge1', 'rougeL'], use_stemmer=True)
    scores = scorer.score(response_original, response_compressed)
    
    return {
        "rouge1_f": scores['rouge1'].fmeasure,
        "rougeL_f": scores['rougeL'].fmeasure,
        "token_reduction": 1 - len(compressed) / len(original),
        "quality_acceptable": scores['rougeL'].fmeasure > 0.85,
    }
```

---

## 🔵 2.1.8 「 Comparison Table (Approaches) 」

| Library | Algorithm | Speed | Quality | GPU Required | Best For |
|---------|-----------|-------|---------|-------------|----------|
| **LLMLingua** | Perplexity scoring | Medium (~150ms) | High (>90% preservation) | Optional (faster) | General prompt compression |
| **LLMLingua-2** | BERT token classification | Fast (~80ms) | Very High (>95%) | Optional | Production workloads |
| **LongLLMLingua** | Query-aware perplexity | Slow (~300ms) | Highest (query-relevant) | Recommended | RAG with long contexts |
| **Selective Context** | Self-information entropy | Fast (~50ms) | Medium (85-90%) | No | CPU-only environments |
| **RECOMP** | Abstractive compression | Slow (~500ms) | High | Yes | Summarization-style compression |
| **Custom Entropy** | Word frequency entropy | Very Fast (~10ms) | Medium (80-85%) | No | High-throughput, low-latency |

---

## 🟠 2.1.9 「 When and Where to Use This Pattern 」

### Use Prompt Compression When:
- Average prompt exceeds **2,000 tokens**
- Context includes retrieved documents (RAG) with filler content
- Cost optimization is a priority (GPT-4 input pricing)
- Latency reduction matters (fewer tokens = faster LLM processing)
- Context window is constrained (e.g., fitting into 8K window)

### Don't Use Compression When:
- Prompts are already concise (< 500 tokens)
- Every token is critical (legal documents, code)
- Compression latency exceeds LLM processing savings
- Quality requirements are absolute (medical, safety-critical)
- You're using a cheap model where token cost is negligible

### Compression Sweet Spot

```
Token Count     Compression Benefit
    < 500       None (skip compression)
  500-2,000     Marginal (5-10% savings)
 2,000-8,000    Good (30-50% savings)
 8,000-32,000   Excellent (50-70% savings)
    > 32,000    Essential (may not fit without it)
```

---

## ✅ 2.1.9b 「 Do's and Don'ts 」

### Do's

| # | Do | Why |
|---|-----|-----|
| 1.1 | Always preserve system instructions uncompressed | Instructions define behavior — compression corrupts them |
| 1.2 | Compress context/documents, not the question | The question is the anchor for relevance scoring |
| 1.3 | Set a minimum token threshold for compression | Below ~500 tokens, overhead exceeds savings |
| 1.4 | Monitor ROUGE scores between compressed/uncompressed responses | Quality regression detection |
| 1.5 | Cache compressed results for repeated queries | Avoid re-compressing identical contexts |
| 1.6 | Use query-aware compression for RAG | Generic compression may remove query-relevant content |
| 1.7 | A/B test compression ratios in production | Optimal ratio varies by use case |
| 1.8 | Fall back to uncompressed on compression error | Availability trumps optimization |

### Don'ts

| # | Don't | Why |
|---|-------|-----|
| 1.1 | Don't compress few-shot examples | Each example teaches the model — removal degrades quality |
| 1.2 | Don't use aggressive ratios without quality monitoring | 0.2 ratio may drop below quality threshold |
| 1.3 | Don't compress code blocks or structured data | Compression breaks syntax and structure |
| 1.4 | Don't assume compression is lossless | Always measure quality impact |
| 1.5 | Don't compress real-time streaming prompts | Compression latency adds to TTFT |
| 1.6 | Don't share compressed prompts across models | Different tokenizers = different optimal compressions |
| 1.7 | Don't skip the baseline measurement | You can't prove savings without before/after data |

---

## ⚪ 2.1.10 「 Pros and Cons 」

| Pros | Cons |
|------|------|
| 30-70% token reduction on long contexts | Adds 50-300ms compression latency |
| Proportional cost savings on input tokens | Risk of removing critical information |
| Faster LLM processing (fewer tokens to read) | Requires quality monitoring infrastructure |
| Can fit more context into limited windows | Compression model requires memory (~500MB) |
| Works with any LLM (model-agnostic) | Different optimal ratios per use case |
| Composable with other optimization patterns | GPU recommended for LLMLingua at scale |
| No changes to downstream prompt template | Compressed text may confuse LLM if too aggressive |

---

## 🟠 2.1.11 「 The DC-Copilot Connection 」

DC-Copilot currently uses a simple truncation approach instead of intelligent compression, which represents a clear optimization opportunity.

### Current State: Truncation at MAX_PROMPT_LENGTH

In `copilot/copilot_api/services/llm_service.py` (line 18), DC-Copilot defines:

```python
MAX_PROMPT_LENGTH = 320000  # characters (~80,000 tokens)
```

Before every LLM call, the prompt is truncated to this limit. This is a **blunt instrument** — it cuts from the end regardless of content importance. If context documents push the prompt past 320K characters, the tail is simply dropped, potentially losing the most relevant content.

### Where Compression Would Help

DC-Copilot's `ContextGatheringNode` (in `routing.py`) retrieves vector context from OpenSearch that often includes multiple document chunks per query. The `_gather_vector_context()` method logs:

```python
self.logger.info(f"Retrieved {doc_citations} document chunks ({doc_length} chars)")
```

When multiple chunks are retrieved (5-10 documents), the total context can reach 50,000-100,000 characters. Much of this is filler — document headers, formatting, repeated metadata. Prompt compression could reduce this by 40-60% while preserving the relevant passages.

### Proposed Integration Point

```python
# In LLMService, before the existing truncation:
class LLMService:
    def _prepare_prompt(self, prompt: str, query: str) -> str:
        """Compress then truncate (compression first to preserve quality)."""
        if len(prompt) > COMPRESSION_THRESHOLD:  # e.g., 50,000 chars
            compressed = self.compressor.compress(
                prompt=prompt,
                question=query,
                target_ratio=0.4,
            )
            prompt = compressed["compressed_prompt"]
        
        # Existing truncation as safety net
        if len(prompt) > MAX_PROMPT_LENGTH:
            prompt = prompt[:MAX_PROMPT_LENGTH]
        
        return prompt
```

### Estimated Impact for DC-Copilot

| Metric | Current (Truncation) | With Compression |
|--------|---------------------|------------------|
| Avg context size | ~60,000 chars | ~24,000 chars (60% reduction) |
| Relevant content preserved | ~70% (tail-cut) | ~95% (entropy-scored) |
| LLM input cost per call | ~$0.075 (GPT-4) | ~$0.030 (GPT-4) |
| Daily savings (10K calls) | — | ~$450/day |
| Compression overhead | 0ms | ~120ms per call |

The compression overhead (120ms) is negligible compared to the LLM call latency (2-5s) and saves ~60% on input token costs.

---

## 🔴 2.1.12 「 Anti-Patterns Gallery 」

### Anti-Pattern 1: "Compress Everything"

```python
# BAD: Compressing short prompts wastes compute
def compress_all_prompts(prompt: str) -> str:
    return compressor.compress(prompt, ratio=0.3)
    # 200-token prompt: 80ms compression to save ~$0.0002
    # Net loss: compression cost > token savings

# GOOD: Only compress when worthwhile
def smart_compress(prompt: str) -> str:
    if estimate_tokens(prompt) < 2000:
        return prompt  # Not worth compressing
    return compressor.compress(prompt, ratio=0.4)
```

### Anti-Pattern 2: "One Ratio Fits All"

```python
# BAD: Same compression for all content types
COMPRESSION_RATIO = 0.3

def compress(prompt: str) -> str:
    return compressor.compress(prompt, ratio=COMPRESSION_RATIO)
    # Code at 0.3 = broken syntax
    # Narratives at 0.3 = fine
    # Instructions at 0.3 = lost critical directives

# GOOD: Content-aware compression
def compress(prompt: str, content_type: str) -> str:
    ratios = {
        "code": 0.9,         # Almost no compression for code
        "instructions": 1.0,  # Never compress instructions
        "documents": 0.4,     # Aggressive for retrieved docs
        "chat_history": 0.5,  # Moderate for conversation
    }
    ratio = ratios.get(content_type, 0.6)
    return compressor.compress(prompt, ratio=ratio)
```

### Anti-Pattern 3: "Compress and Forget"

```python
# BAD: No quality monitoring
def process_query(query: str, context: str) -> str:
    compressed = compressor.compress(context)
    return llm.invoke(compressed)
    # No way to know if compression degraded quality
    # Silent quality regression over weeks

# GOOD: Track quality metrics
def process_query(query: str, context: str) -> str:
    compressed = compressor.compress(context)
    response = llm.invoke(compressed)
    
    metrics.track("compression.ratio", compressed.ratio)
    metrics.track("compression.tokens_saved", compressed.tokens_saved)
    
    if random.random() < 0.01:  # 1% sampling
        uncompressed_response = llm.invoke(context)
        quality = compute_rouge_l(response, uncompressed_response)
        metrics.track("compression.quality_score", quality)
    
    return response
```

### Anti-Pattern 4: "Compress the Question"

```python
# BAD: Compressing the user's question
def compress_prompt(instruction: str, context: str, question: str) -> str:
    full = f"{instruction}\n{context}\n{question}"
    return compressor.compress(full, ratio=0.3)
    # The question anchors LLM focus — compressing it = random behavior

# GOOD: Segment-aware compression
def compress_prompt(instruction: str, context: str, question: str) -> str:
    compressed_context = compressor.compress(
        context,
        ratio=0.4,
        instruction=instruction,  # Preserved as-is
        question=question,        # Preserved as-is, used for relevance scoring
    )
    return f"{instruction}\n{compressed_context}\n{question}"
```

## ⚪ 2.1.13 「 Performance Benchmark Table 」

| Metric | LLMLingua | LLMLingua-2 | LongLLMLingua | Selective Context | Custom Entropy |
|--------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Compression time (1K tokens)** | 45ms | 25ms | 120ms | 8ms | 3ms |
| **Compression time (10K tokens)** | 180ms | 95ms | 350ms | 45ms | 12ms |
| **Compression time (50K tokens)** | 850ms | 420ms | 1,600ms | 180ms | 50ms |
| **Quality @ 0.5 ratio (ROUGE-L)** | 0.92 | 0.95 | 0.96 | 0.87 | 0.82 |
| **Quality @ 0.3 ratio (ROUGE-L)** | 0.85 | 0.89 | 0.93 | 0.78 | 0.71 |
| **Memory (model loaded)** | 1.2GB | 500MB | 2.5GB | 50MB | 10MB |
| **GPU benefit** | 3x faster | 2x faster | 5x faster | None | None |
| **Max input length** | ~100K tokens | ~100K tokens | ~200K tokens | Unlimited | Unlimited |
| **Multilingual** | Limited | Yes (BERT) | Limited | Language-agnostic | Language-agnostic |

## 🔵 2.1.14 「 Decision Flowchart 」

```
╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║          COMPRESSION LIBRARY SELECTION FLOWCHART              ║
╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║                                                               ║
║  START: "Should I compress prompts?"                         ║

║     YES │          │ NO                                       ║
║         │          ▼                                          ║
║         │   ┌────────────────┐                               ║
║         │   │ No compression │                               ║
║         │   │ needed         │                               ║
║         │   └────────────────┘                               ║
║         ▼                                                     ║
║  ┌──────────────────────┐                                    ║
║  │ GPU available?        │                                    ║

║     YES │          │ NO                                       ║
║         ▼          ▼                                          ║
║  ┌────────────┐ ┌────────────────────┐                       ║
║  │ RAG with   │ │ Selective Context  │                       ║
║  │ long docs? │ │ or Custom Entropy  │                       ║
║  └──┬─────┬───┘ └────────────────────┘                       ║
║  YES│     │NO                                                 ║
║     ▼     ▼                                                   ║
║  ┌──────┐ ┌──────────┐                                       ║
║  │Long  │ │LLMLingua │                                       ║
║  │LLM   │ │    -2    │                                       ║
║  │Lingua│ └──────────┘                                       ║
║  └──────┘                                                     ║
╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
```

---

## 🟢 2.1.15 「 Migration Path 」

### Step 0 → Measure Current Token Usage

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ BASELINE MEASUREMENT                         │
│                                              │
│  ☐ Instrument all LLM calls with token count│
│  ☐ Log prompt length distribution (p50/p95)  │
│  ☐ Calculate monthly token cost              │
│  ☐ Identify top 5 highest-token endpoints    │
│  ☐ Sample 100 prompts for quality baseline   │
│  ☐ Measure response quality without compress │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Step 1 → Add Compression to Highest-Volume Endpoint

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ PILOT COMPRESSION                            │
│                                              │
│  ☐ Install compression library               │
│  ☐ Add compression node to LangGraph         │
│  ☐ Route 10% of traffic through compression  │
│  ☐ Monitor: latency delta, quality delta     │
│  ☐ A/B test compressed vs. uncompressed      │
│  ☐ Tune ratio based on quality scores        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Step 2 → Roll Out to All Endpoints

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ PRODUCTION ROLLOUT                           │
│                                              │
│  ☐ Configure per-endpoint compression ratios │
│  ☐ Set up quality monitoring dashboard       │
│  ☐ Create alert on quality score < threshold │
│  ☐ Implement compression cache for repeats   │
│  ☐ Document compression config in runbook    │
│  ☐ Ramp to 100% traffic over 2 weeks        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Step 3 → Optimize and Iterate

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ CONTINUOUS OPTIMIZATION                      │
│                                              │
│  ☐ Monthly review of compression metrics     │
│  ☐ Adjust ratios as content patterns change  │
│  ☐ Evaluate newer compression libraries      │
│  ☐ Consider adaptive compression if static   │
│     ratios leave quality on the table        │
│  ☐ Track cumulative cost savings vs. goal    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## 💡 2.1.16 「 Interview / Viva Q&A 」

**Q1: How does LLMLingua decide which tokens to remove?**

**A:** LLMLingua uses a small language model (GPT-2 or similar) to compute the perplexity of each token in the prompt. Tokens with low perplexity (highly predictable given surrounding context) are removed first, since the target LLM can reconstruct them from context. Tokens with high perplexity (surprising, information-dense) are kept. The algorithm operates in two passes: coarse-grained (sentence-level filtering) then fine-grained (token-level within kept sentences). The `rate` parameter controls how aggressively to compress — a rate of 0.3 keeps ~30% of tokens. The key insight is that natural language is highly redundant — roughly 50-70% of tokens in typical text carry minimal information.

**Q2: When does compression overhead exceed its benefit?**

**A:** The break-even point depends on three variables: compression time, tokens saved, and per-token LLM cost. For GPT-4 at $0.01/1K input tokens and LLMLingua-2 at ~25ms per 1K tokens: compressing a 500-token prompt saves ~$0.0025 but costs ~12ms compute. At 10K requests/day, that's $25/day savings vs. ~2 minutes total compute. The break-even is around 1,000-2,000 tokens depending on the model. Below that, skip compression. For cheaper models (GPT-3.5, Claude Haiku), the break-even rises to ~5,000 tokens because per-token savings are smaller.

**Q3: How do you handle compression quality regression in production?**

**A:** Three layers of defense. First, sample-based monitoring: run 1% of requests through both compressed and uncompressed paths, compute ROUGE-L between responses, and alert if the score drops below 0.85. Second, content-type routing: route code blocks, structured data, and instructions around compression — only compress retrieved documents and conversation history. Third, adaptive fallback: if a specific query's compressed response scores below threshold, retry with uncompressed prompt and log the failure for analysis. The cost of the 1% sampling is negligible — at 10K requests/day, that's 100 extra LLM calls (~$1/day for quality assurance).

**Q4: What's the difference between prompt compression and RAG chunking?**

**A:** They operate at different stages. RAG chunking decides _which_ documents to retrieve — it's about selection from a corpus. Prompt compression decides _how much_ of the retrieved documents to keep — it's about reduction within a prompt. They're complementary: first, retrieve the top-K relevant chunks (RAG), then compress those chunks to remove filler (compression). The optimal workflow is: retrieve 10 chunks → compress to 3,000 tokens → send to LLM. Without compression, you might need to retrieve only 3 chunks to fit the context window, missing potentially relevant information.

**Q5: Can prompt compression break multi-step reasoning?**

**A:** Yes, if critical chain-of-thought elements are in compressed-away tokens. This is why instruction segments and few-shot examples should never be compressed — they define the reasoning pattern. For context documents, compression rarely breaks reasoning because the LLM uses them as reference, not as reasoning steps. The risk is highest with highly compressed ratios (< 0.3) on technical content where specific numbers, formulas, or step sequences matter. Mitigate by using query-aware compression (LongLLMLingua) which preserves tokens relevant to the specific question, and by setting minimum retention for sentences containing numbers or technical terms.

---

## ✅ 2.1.17 「 Production Readiness Checklist 」

- [ ] Measured baseline token usage across all endpoints (p50, p95, p99)
- [ ] Selected compression library based on latency/quality requirements
- [ ] Implemented compression as a separate node in the LangGraph pipeline
- [ ] Configured per-content-type compression ratios (instructions=1.0, docs=0.4)
- [ ] Set minimum token threshold below which compression is skipped
- [ ] Implemented quality monitoring with ROUGE-L sampling (≥1% of traffic)
- [ ] Set up alerts for quality score drops below threshold
- [ ] Added compression metrics to observability dashboard
- [ ] Tested fallback path (compression failure → uncompressed)
- [ ] A/B tested compression vs. no compression for 1 week
- [ ] Documented compression configuration in operational runbook
- [ ] Verified compression works across all supported languages (if multilingual)

---

## 🟡 2.1.18 「 Troubleshooting Guide 」

| Symptom | Check | Fix |
|---------|-------|-----|
| Quality drops after enabling compression | Compression ratio too aggressive | Increase ratio from 0.3 to 0.5, monitor ROUGE-L |
| Compression slower than expected | Running on CPU without batching | Enable GPU or switch to LLMLingua-2 (BERT-based) |
| Out of memory loading compression model | Model too large for instance | Use Selective Context (50MB) or upgrade instance |
| Compressed output is garbled | Token-level compression on structured data | Skip compression for code, JSON, tables |
| Inconsistent compression ratios | Dynamic content length variation | Use adaptive compression with quality feedback loop |
| LLM ignores compressed context | Too much content removed | Preserve first/last sentences, increase ratio |
| Compression cache not effective | Each query has unique context | Cache by document ID, not full prompt |
| Compression increases total latency | Small prompts being compressed | Set min_tokens_to_compress ≥ 2000 |
| Different results on same prompt | Non-deterministic compression model | Set seed/temperature=0 on compression model |
| Quality good on English, bad on other languages | Monolingual compression model | Use LLMLingua-2 (multilingual BERT) |

---

## 💰 2.1.19 「 Cost Calculator 」

### Compression ROI Calculator

| Scale | Daily Calls | Avg Tokens | Uncompressed Cost/mo | Compressed Cost/mo | Savings/mo | Infra Cost/mo |
|-------|-----------|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|-----------|-------------|
| **1K/day** | 1,000 | 5,000 | $375 (GPT-4o) | $150 | $225 | $50 (CPU) |
| **10K/day** | 10,000 | 5,000 | $3,750 | $1,500 | $2,250 | $150 (GPU) |
| **100K/day** | 100,000 | 5,000 | $37,500 | $15,000 | $22,500 | $500 (GPU) |

### Compression Library Infrastructure Costs

| Library | CPU Instance | GPU Instance | Memory | Monthly Cost |
|---------|------------|------------|--------|-------------|
| LLMLingua | t3.large ($60/mo) | g4dn.xlarge ($380/mo) | 1.2GB | $60-380 |
| LLMLingua-2 | t3.medium ($30/mo) | g4dn.xlarge ($380/mo) | 500MB | $30-380 |
| Selective Context | t3.small ($15/mo) | N/A | 50MB | $15 |
| Custom Entropy | t3.micro ($8/mo) | N/A | 10MB | $8 |

> **Key insight:** At 10K requests/day with 5K-token average prompts, compression saves ~$2,250/month on LLM costs while adding ~$150/month in infrastructure. That's a 15:1 return. The ROI improves at scale — at 100K/day, it's 45:1. The break-even point is approximately 200 requests/day at 5K tokens average.

║  └──────┬───────┘                                    ║
║         │                                             ║
║         ▼                                             ║

║       │                                               ║

## 🔢 2.1.23 「 Real-World Scale Numbers 」

### At 10K Requests/Day

| Metric | Without Compression | With Compression (0.4 ratio) |
|--------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| **Daily input tokens** | 50M tokens | 20M tokens |
| **Daily LLM input cost (GPT-4o)** | $125 | $50 |
| **Monthly LLM input cost** | $3,750 | $1,500 |
| **Avg LLM processing time** | 3.2s | 2.1s |
| **Compression compute cost/mo** | $0 | $150 |
| **Net monthly savings** | — | $2,100 |

### At 100K Requests/Day

| Metric | Without Compression | With Compression (0.4 ratio) |
|--------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| **Daily input tokens** | 500M tokens | 200M tokens |
| **Daily LLM input cost (GPT-4o)** | $1,250 | $500 |
| **Monthly LLM input cost** | $37,500 | $15,000 |
| **Avg LLM processing time** | 3.5s | 2.2s |
| **Compression compute cost/mo** | $0 | $500 |
| **Net monthly savings** | — | $22,000 |

- **Key insight:** At 100K requests/day, prompt compression yields $22,000/month in net savings (after infrastructure costs). But the bigger win is latency: processing 60% fewer tokens means ~37% faster LLM responses (3.5s → 2.2s p50). For real-time applications, this latency reduction is often more valuable than cost savings. The break-even volume is ~200 requests/day — below that, the infrastructure cost exceeds token savings. Above that, every additional request amplifies the ROI. At DC-Copilot scale, the `MAX_PROMPT_LENGTH = 320000` truncation could be replaced with compression to preserve relevant content while staying within token budgets.
