## 🟢 2.3.7 「 How to Implement (Production Guide) 」

### Step 1: Profile Your Current Token Usage

```python
import tiktoken

def profile_prompt_usage(
    system_prompt: str,
    conversation: str,
    documents: str,
    user_query: str,
    model: str = "gpt-4",
) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Profile token usage across prompt sections."""
    enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(model)
    sections = {
        "system_prompt": len(enc.encode(system_prompt)),
        "conversation": len(enc.encode(conversation)),
        "documents": len(enc.encode(documents)),
        "user_query": len(enc.encode(user_query)),
    }
    total = sum(sections.values())
    return {
        "sections": sections,
        "total": total,
        "percentages": {k: f"{v/total:.1%}" for k, v in sections.items()},
    }
```

### Step 2: Define Budget Allocation Policy

| Section | Min | Max | Overflow Priority |
|---------|-----|-----|-------------------|
| System prompt (core) | 200 | 800 | 4 (trim examples only) |
| User query | 100 | 2,000 | 5 (never trim) |
| Conversation history | 200 | 4,000 | 2 (summarise) |
| Retrieved documents | 0 | remaining | 1 (drop least relevant) |

### Step 3: Implement Token Counting

```python
# CRITICAL: Use model-specific tokenizer, not len(text)
# "Hello world" is 2 tokens, not 11 characters
# CJK text: 1 character ≈ 2-3 tokens (not 1)
# URLs: 1 URL ≈ 10-30 tokens

# Wrong:
estimated_tokens = len(text) // 4  # ❌ 4x error on CJK

# Right:
import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4")
actual_tokens = len(enc.encode(text))  # ✅ Exact
```

### Step 4: Implement Overflow Handling

```python
def trim_to_budget(
    sections: List[Tuple[str, int, str]],  # (name, priority, content)
    budget: int,
    tokenizer,
) -> Dict[str, str]:
    """Trim sections to fit budget, lowest priority first."""
    # Sort by priority ascending (lowest trimmed first)
    sections.sort(key=lambda s: s[1])
    
    total = sum(len(tokenizer.encode(s[2])) for s in sections)
    overflow = total - budget
    
    result = {}
    for name, priority, content in sections:
        if overflow > 0 and priority < 5:  # never trim priority 5
            tokens = tokenizer.encode(content)
            trim_amount = min(len(tokens), overflow)
            tokens = tokens[:len(tokens) - trim_amount]
            content = tokenizer.decode(tokens)
            overflow -= trim_amount
        result[name] = content
    
    return result
```

### Step 5: Monitor and Tune

Track these metrics per request:
- **Budget utilisation** — aim for 70-90% (too low = wasted capacity, too high = frequent overflow)
- **Overflow frequency** — how often trimming is triggered
- **Dropped document count** — how many relevant docs are excluded
- **Summary compression ratio** — tokens saved by summarisation

---

## 🔵 2.3.8 「 Comparison Table (Approaches) 」

| Strategy | Description | Token Efficiency | Implementation | Best For |
|----------|------------|-----------------|----------------|----------|
| **Fixed allocation** | Static token budgets per section | Medium | ⭐ | Predictable workloads |
| **Proportional allocation** | Budgets scale with content availability | High | ⭐⭐ | Variable content sizes |
| **Priority-based greedy** | Fill by relevance until budget exhausted | Very High | ⭐⭐⭐ | RAG with many docs |
| **Sliding window** | Keep recent N messages, summarise rest | Medium | ⭐⭐ | Multi-turn chat |
| **Recursive summarisation** | Hierarchical summaries of summaries | High | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | Very long conversations |
| **Truncation** | Hard cut at character/token limit | Low | ⭐ | Emergency fallback only |

---

## 🟠 2.3.9 「 When and Where to Use This Pattern 」

### Use When

| Scenario | Why |
|----------|-----|
| **Multi-source RAG** | Multiple document types compete for window space |
| **Long conversations** | History grows unboundedly without management |
| **Large system prompts** | Complex instructions consume significant budget |
| **Multi-model deployment** | Different models have different window sizes |
| **Cost-sensitive apps** | Wasted tokens = wasted money at every request |

### Skip When

| Scenario | Why |
|----------|-----|
| **Short single-turn Q&A** | Query + response fits easily; no budget pressure |
| **Small system prompts** | < 200 tokens system prompt; no competition for space |
| **200K+ context models** | Modern models (Claude 200K, Gemini 1M) rarely overflow |
| **Prototype / MVP stage** | Premature optimization; ship first, optimize later |

---

## ✅ 2.3.9b 「 Do's and Don'ts 」

### ✅ Do's

| # | Practice | Reason |
|---|----------|--------|
| 1.1 | Use model-specific tokenizer (tiktoken) | Character-based estimates can be 2-4x wrong |
| 1.2 | Reserve safety margin (2-5% of window) | Prevents off-by-one overflows |
| 1.3 | Reserve output token budget | Input + output must fit in window |
| 1.4 | Log budget utilisation per request | Enables data-driven tuning |
| 1.5 | Trim documents before conversation history | History has temporal context; docs are fungible |
| 1.6 | Keep at least 2 recent turns uncompressed | Model needs recent context for coherent responses |

### ❌ Don'ts

| # | Anti-Practice | Consequence |
|---|--------------|-------------|
| 1.1 | Estimate tokens as `len(text) / 4` | 2-4x error on non-English, URLs, code |
| 1.2 | Truncate mid-sentence | Model gets confused by incomplete context |
| 1.3 | Drop user query to fit budget | Model can't answer a question it can't see |
| 1.4 | Ignore output token reservation | Input fills window, no room for response |
| 1.5 | Use same budget for all models | 4K model needs aggressive trimming; 128K doesn't |
| 1.6 | Trim system prompt first | Model loses behavior instructions, goes off-rails |

---

## ⚪ 2.3.10 「 Pros and Cons 」

| Dimension | Pro | Con |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Correctness** | Model always sees most relevant context | Budget allocation heuristics may drop needed info |
| **Cost** | No wasted tokens on irrelevant content | Token counting adds ~1ms overhead per request |
| **Reliability** | Never exceeds model window (no API errors) | Aggressive trimming can degrade response quality |
| **Flexibility** | Adapts to different content sizes per request | Requires tuning per use case and model |
| **Observability** | Budget reports show exactly what's in/out | Adds monitoring infrastructure complexity |
| **Scalability** | Same approach works 4K → 200K windows | Budget ratios need recalibration per model |

---

## 🟠 2.3.11 「 The DC-Copilot Connection 」

### Current Implementation: Two-Layer Window Management

DC-Copilot manages its context window through two complementary mechanisms:

**Layer 1: Hard Truncation in LLMService**

The `LLMService` in [llm_service.py:18](copilot/copilot_api/services/llm_service.py#L18) enforces a hard ceiling:

```python
MAX_PROMPT_LENGTH = 320000  # characters (~80,000 tokens)
```

Before every LLM call, the assembled prompt is truncated at 320,000 characters. This is a blunt safety net — it prevents API errors but doesn't optimize what content survives truncation.

**Layer 2: Intelligent Summarisation via langmem**

The `SummarizationNodeWrapper` in [summarization.py:90](copilot/copilot_api/nodes/summarization.py#L90) provides intelligent context window management:

```python
# From config/constants.py
SUMMARY_MAX_TOKENS = 4000              # Target max conversation history
SUMMARY_MAX_TOKENS_BEFORE_SUMMARY = 2000  # Trigger threshold (50% of max)
SUMMARY_MAX_SUMMARY_TOKENS = 512       # Max summary size
```

When conversation history exceeds 2,000 tokens, langmem's `SummarizationNode` generates a running summary. Key design decisions:

1. **Parallel execution** — summarisation runs in `SummarizationSubgraph` alongside `CopilotFlow` (see [state_machine.py:88](copilot/copilot_api/graph/state_machine.py#L88)), so it never blocks the response path
2. **Incremental summaries** — builds on prior summary (`EXISTING_SUMMARY_PROMPT`) instead of regenerating from scratch each turn
3. **Message trimming** — emits `RemoveMessage` objects ([summarization.py:143](copilot/copilot_api/nodes/summarization.py#L143)) that the parent graph's `add_messages` reducer uses to delete old messages from the DynamoDB checkpoint
4. **Question preservation** — custom prompts explicitly instruct the model to preserve user questions verbatim in the summary, ensuring "what was my first question?" can be answered even after trimming

### Architecture: How the Two Layers Interact

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  DC-Copilot Context Window Stack                            │
│                                                             │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ Layer 2: SummarizationNodeWrapper (Smart)           │   │
│  │ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │   │
│  │ │ Max history: 4,000 tokens                   │     │   │
│  │ │ Trigger:     2,000 tokens (50%)             │     │   │
│  │ │ Summary cap: 512 tokens                     │     │   │
│  │ │ Strategy:    Incremental, question-preserving│     │   │
│  │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                        │                                    │
│                        ▼                                    │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ Layer 1: MAX_PROMPT_LENGTH Truncation (Blunt)       │   │
│  │ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │   │
│  │ │ Hard ceiling: 320,000 chars (~80K tokens)   │     │   │
│  │ │ Strategy:     Right-truncation at limit      │     │   │
│  │ │ Purpose:      Safety net for edge cases      │     │   │
│  │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                        │                                    │
│                        ▼                                    │
│              Azure OpenAI (128K window)                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Potential Enhancement: Token-Aware Budget Allocation

Currently, DC-Copilot doesn't budget tokens across prompt sections. The retrieved `docs_context` from `ContextGatheringNode` can vary from 200 to 50,000+ tokens depending on the OpenSearch results. A `TokenBudgetManager` between context gathering and prompt assembly would ensure:

- System instructions always fit
- Work order briefing gets priority over general docs
- Conversation summary is never crowded out by large document retrievals

---

## 🔴 2.3.12 「 Anti-Patterns Gallery 」

### Anti-Pattern 1: Character-Based Token Estimation

```python
# ❌ WRONG: "4 chars per token" is folklore, not fact
def fits_in_window(text: str, max_tokens: int = 128_000) -> bool:
    estimated_tokens = len(text) // 4
    return estimated_tokens < max_tokens

# This passes for Korean text that actually uses 150K tokens:
fits_in_window("한국어 텍스트" * 30000, 128_000)  # True! But wrong.
# Korean: 1 character ≈ 2-3 tokens, not 0.25

# ✅ RIGHT: Use actual tokenizer
import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4")
def fits_in_window(text: str, max_tokens: int = 128_000) -> bool:
    return len(enc.encode(text)) < max_tokens
```

### Anti-Pattern 2: First-In-First-Dropped for Documents

```python
# ❌ WRONG: Dropping oldest documents regardless of relevance
def trim_documents(docs: List[str], budget: int) -> List[str]:
    result = []
    used = 0
    for doc in docs:  # Ordered by retrieval time, not relevance!
        tokens = count_tokens(doc)
        if used + tokens <= budget:
            result.append(doc)
            used += tokens
    return result

# ✅ RIGHT: Drop by relevance score (least relevant first)
def trim_documents(docs: List[Tuple[str, float]], budget: int) -> List[str]:
    # Sort by relevance descending
    docs_sorted = sorted(docs, key=lambda d: d[1], reverse=True)
    result = []
    used = 0
    for doc, score in docs_sorted:
        tokens = count_tokens(doc)
        if used + tokens <= budget:
            result.append(doc)
            used += tokens
    return result
```

### Anti-Pattern 3: Ignoring Output Token Reservation

```python
# ❌ WRONG: Filling entire 128K window with input
max_input = 128_000  # No room left for output!

# ✅ RIGHT: Reserve output budget
MODEL_WINDOW = 128_000
MAX_OUTPUT = 4_096
SAFETY_MARGIN = 2_000
max_input = MODEL_WINDOW - MAX_OUTPUT - SAFETY_MARGIN  # 121,904
```

### Anti-Pattern 4: Static Budget on Multi-Model System

```python
# ❌ WRONG: Same budget for all models
TOKEN_BUDGET = 4_000  # Works for GPT-3.5-turbo-4k, wastes 96% of GPT-4-128k

# ✅ RIGHT: Model-aware budgets
MODEL_BUDGETS = {
    "gpt-3.5-turbo": {"window": 4_096, "max_input": 2_000},
    "gpt-4": {"window": 128_000, "max_input": 121_904},
    "claude-3.5-sonnet": {"window": 200_000, "max_input": 191_904},
}
```

---

## ⚪ 2.3.13 「 Performance Benchmark Table 」

| Operation | Time | Notes |
|-----------|------|-------|
| **tiktoken encode** (100 tokens) | 0.02ms | Negligible |
| **tiktoken encode** (10K tokens) | 0.8ms | Still fast |
| **tiktoken encode** (100K tokens) | 7ms | Acceptable |
| **Budget allocation** (5 slots) | <0.01ms | Pure arithmetic |
| **Priority sort** (50 chunks) | <0.01ms | Python sort |
| **Greedy fill** (50 chunks) | 0.1ms | Linear scan |
| **Sliding window summarise** | 800-2000ms | LLM call for summary |
| **Total overhead** (no summarise) | 1-8ms | ~0.1% of LLM latency |
| **Total overhead** (with summarise) | 800-2008ms | Dominated by LLM summary call |

| Window Management Strategy | Tokens Saved | Latency Added | Quality Impact |
|---------------------------|-------------|---------------|----------------|
| No management (raw) | 0% | 0ms | Baseline (risk overflow) |
| Hard truncation | 10-30% | <1ms | Moderate quality loss |
| Budget allocation | 20-40% | 1-5ms | Minimal quality loss |
| Sliding window + summary | 40-70% | 800-2000ms | Good (preserves key context) |
| Priority greedy + summary | 50-80% | 800-2005ms | Best (relevance-optimized) |

```
                                              ▼             ▼
```

## 🟢 2.3.15 「 Migration Path 」

### Step 0 → Baseline Measurement

```
Instrument your current system:
- Log total prompt tokens per request
- Log per-section token counts (system, history, docs, query)
- Track overflow/truncation frequency
- Measure: what percentage of your requests hit > 50% window?
Duration: 1 week.
```

### Step 1 → Add Token Counting + Safety Net

```
Replace character-based estimates with tiktoken.
Add hard safety margin (2% of window).
Reserve output token budget explicitly.
Add overflow logging (alert when > 80% utilisation).
Expected improvement: zero API overflow errors.
Duration: 1 sprint.
```

### Step 2 → Implement Budget Allocation

```
Define budget slots per prompt section.
Implement priority-based overflow trimming.
Add per-request budget utilisation logging.
Tune slot sizes based on 1-week production data.
Expected improvement: 20-30% fewer wasted tokens.
Duration: 1-2 sprints.
```

### Step 3 → Add Sliding Window Summarisation

```
Implement running summary for conversation history.
Parallelize summarisation (don't block response path).
Add summary quality monitoring (information retention score).
Expected improvement: support 10x longer conversations.
Duration: 2-3 sprints.
```

---

## 💡 2.3.16 「 Interview / Viva Q&A 」

**Q1: Why can't you just use the biggest context window available and skip window management?**

> Bigger windows don't eliminate the problem — they move the goalpost. Even with 200K tokens, a production system with verbose system prompts, 20 retrieved documents, and a 50-turn conversation will eventually overflow. Additionally, larger contexts increase latency (attention is O(n²) in standard transformers) and cost (you pay per input token). Window management ensures you use tokens efficiently regardless of window size, giving the model the highest signal-to-noise context possible.

**Q2: How do you handle the "lost in the middle" problem with long contexts?**

> Research (Liu et al., 2023) shows LLMs attend most to content at the beginning and end of the context, with weaker attention to the middle. To mitigate: (1) place the most important content (user query, key instructions) at the beginning and end of the prompt, (2) put retrieved documents in relevance-descending order, (3) use structured section headers so the model can "navigate" the context, (4) keep total context as short as possible — a shorter, more relevant context outperforms a longer, noisier one.

**Q3: Should token counting happen synchronously on the request path?**

> Yes, but it's not a bottleneck. tiktoken encodes 100K tokens in ~7ms — negligible compared to 2-5 second LLM calls. The cost of NOT counting (and getting an API overflow error or wasting tokens) far exceeds 7ms. Cache the tokenizer instance (it's thread-safe), and if you're encoding the same strings repeatedly, add an LRU cache on the encode call.

**Q4: How do you decide the right budget split between conversation history and retrieved documents?**

> Start with a 20/80 split (20% history, 80% documents) for single-turn or few-turn interactions. Shift to 40/60 for multi-turn conversations where context continuity matters. Measure what predicts response quality — if history drops correlate with quality degradation, increase history budget. If document drops correlate with hallucination, increase document budget. The optimal split is domain-specific and requires A/B testing.

**Q5: What happens when the user's query itself is very long (e.g., a pasted log file)?**

> Long user queries are a special case. Options: (1) never truncate the user query (safest, may leave no room for docs), (2) extract the actual question from the log paste and summarise the log into key points, (3) use a two-pass approach — first call summarises the log, second call answers the question with the summary. Option 2 is most practical. Some systems impose a max query length (e.g., 4,000 tokens) and reject longer inputs with a helpful error.

---

## ✅ 2.3.17 「 Production Readiness Checklist 」

- [ ] Token counting uses model-specific tokenizer (tiktoken / sentencepiece)
- [ ] Output token budget explicitly reserved (not part of input budget)
- [ ] Safety margin of 2-5% applied to prevent edge-case overflows
- [ ] Budget slots defined for all prompt sections with min/max limits
- [ ] Overflow policy implemented (priority-based trimming, not random)
- [ ] User query is never truncated or dropped
- [ ] Conversation history has sliding window with summarisation
- [ ] Retrieved documents ranked by relevance before budget allocation
- [ ] Per-request budget utilisation logged for monitoring
- [ ] Overflow events logged with alert threshold (> 5% of requests)
- [ ] Multi-model support with per-model budget configuration
- [ ] Load-tested at 2x peak volume to validate budget allocation latency

---

## 🟡 2.3.18 「 Troubleshooting Guide 」

| Symptom | Check | Fix |
|---------|-------|-----|
| API returns "context length exceeded" | Token count exceeds model window | Add safety margin; enforce hard cap before API call |
| Model ignores instructions | System prompt truncated by overflow | Increase system prompt priority; reduce doc budget |
| Model hallucinates on RAG queries | Relevant docs dropped by budget constraint | Increase doc budget; improve relevance scoring |
| Response quality degrades over conversation | History summarised too aggressively | Increase history budget; lower summary trigger |
| Tokenizer returns wrong counts | Using wrong model name for tiktoken | Match tokenizer to deployed model exactly |
| Budget utilisation always < 30% | Budgets too generous for actual content | Tighten budgets; use proportional allocation |
| Budget utilisation always at 100% | Budgets too tight; constant overflow | Increase model window size or compress inputs |
| CJK text overflows unexpectedly | Using character-based estimation | Switch to tiktoken (CJK = 2-3 tokens/char) |
| Summarisation too slow | LLM summary call on critical path | Move to parallel subgraph (DC-Copilot pattern) |
| Different results on same query | Non-deterministic budget allocation | Fix sort order for equal-relevance chunks |

---

## 💰 2.3.19 「 Cost Calculator 」

### Token Waste Without Window Management

| Waste Source | Tokens Wasted/Request | At 10K req/day | Monthly Cost (GPT-4) |
|-------------|----------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Redundant history | ~1,500 | 15M | $450 |
| Low-relevance docs | ~2,000 | 20M | $600 |
| Oversized system prompt | ~300 | 3M | $90 |
| **Total waste** | **~3,800** | **38M** | **$1,140** |

### Savings With Window Management

| Strategy | Tokens Saved/Request | At 10K req/day | Monthly Savings |
|----------|---------------------|----------------|----------------|
| Budget allocation only | 1,500 | 15M/day | $450/mo |
| + Sliding window | 2,500 | 25M/day | $750/mo |
| + Document prioritisation | 3,200 | 32M/day | $960/mo |
| + System prompt optimisation | 3,500 | 35M/day | $1,050/mo |

### At Scale

| Scale | Queries/day | Monthly waste (no mgmt) | Monthly savings (full mgmt) | ROI |
|-------|-------------|------------------------|-----------------------------|-----|
| **Small** | 1K | $114 | $105 | 92% reduction |
| **Medium** | 10K | $1,140 | $1,050 | 92% reduction |
| **Large** | 100K | $11,400 | $10,500 | 92% reduction |


---

## ⚪ 2.3.22 「 Dependency Matrix 」

| Relationship | Topic | Why |
|-------------|-------|-----|
| **Requires** | Prompt Compression (Ch 2.1) | Compressed prompts use fewer tokens, leaving more budget for documents |
| **Requires** | Message Compaction (Ch 2.4) | Compacted history fits in smaller budget allocation |
| **Enhances** | Semantic Caching (Ch 2.2) | Smaller contexts are faster to embed for cache lookup |
| **Feeds into** | Cost Dashboard (Ch 2.10) | Budget utilisation is a key cost metric |
| **Works with** | Token-Aware Chunking (Ch 2.8) | Chunks sized to fit budget slots avoid mid-chunk truncation |
| **Works with** | Model Routing (Ch 2.6) | Different models have different windows; budget must adapt |
| **Relates to** | Structured Output (Ch 2.5) | Structured output reduces response tokens, freeing more input budget |
| **Depends on** | Subgraph Composition (Ch 1.8) | Parallel summarisation (DC-Copilot pattern) removes summary from critical path |

---

## 🔢 2.3.23 「 Real-World Scale Numbers 」

### At 10K Queries/Day

| Metric | No Window Mgmt | With Full Mgmt | Delta |
|--------|---------------|----------------|-------|
| Avg input tokens/request | 8,500 | 5,100 | -40% |
| Tokens wasted/request | 3,800 | 300 | -92% |
| API overflow errors/day | 2.5 | 0 | -100% |
| Avg response quality (1-5) | 3.8 | 4.2 | +10% |
| Monthly input cost (GPT-4) | $2,550 | $1,530 | -$1,020 |
| P99 latency | 9,200ms | 6,400ms | -30% |

### At 100K Queries/Day

| Metric | No Window Mgmt | With Full Mgmt | Delta |
|--------|---------------|----------------|-------|
| Avg input tokens/request | 12,000 | 6,800 | -43% |
| Tokens wasted/request | 5,200 | 400 | -92% |
| API overflow errors/day | 250 | 0 | -100% |
| Monthly input cost (GPT-4) | $36,000 | $20,400 | -$15,600 |
| Monthly summary LLM cost | $0 | $800 | +$800 |
| Net monthly savings | — | — | $14,800 |
| P99 latency | 14,000ms | 8,200ms | -41% |

> **Key insight:** Context window management delivers compound benefits — fewer wasted tokens reduces cost, smaller prompts reduce latency, and higher signal-to-noise ratio improves response quality. The 92% waste reduction is consistent across scales because the waste sources (redundant history, irrelevant docs, verbose system prompts) are structural, not volume-dependent. At 100K queries/day, this single optimization saves ~$15K/month — more than the cost of a mid-level engineer.
