---

# 💰「 Chapter 2.6 」— Model Routing / Cascading

> <span style="color:#00D4FF">**Token & Prompt Reduction**</span> · Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐ · Read Time: ~25 min

**Why waste a $60/M-token model on "Hello, how are you?"** Model routing inspects every request — complexity, token budget, latency SLA, sensitivity — then directs it to the cheapest model that can still produce an acceptable answer. Cascading adds a second dimension: start cheap, escalate to expensive only when confidence is low. Together they cut inference costs 40-70% without measurable quality loss on the easy tail of your traffic.

---

## 🔵 2.6.1 「 Also Known As 」

| Alias | Context |
|-------|---------|
| **Model Cascading** | Sequential fallback: try small model first, escalate on low confidence |
| **LLM Routing** | Request-level dispatch to different model endpoints |
| **Tiered Inference** | Organizing models into cost/quality tiers |
| **Model Switching** | Dynamic model selection based on runtime signals |
| **Adaptive Model Selection** | ML-based router that learns optimal routing over time |
| **Cost-Aware Dispatch** | Routing decisions weighted by per-token pricing |
| **Quality-Gated Escalation** | Cascade with explicit quality thresholds between tiers |
| **Model Multiplexing** | Single API surface backed by multiple models |
| **Smart Fallback** | Cascade framed as graceful degradation |
| **Inference Tiering** | Infrastructure-level model pool management |

---

## 🔵 2.6.2 「 The Layman Explanation 」

Imagine a hospital triage desk. A patient with a paper cut doesn't need a brain surgeon — a nurse handles it in seconds. A patient with chest pain gets the cardiologist immediately. The triage desk doesn't treat anyone; it **routes** each case to the right provider based on severity.

Model routing works the same way:

- **Simple questions** ("What's the weather?") → small, fast, cheap model (GPT-4o-mini, Claude Haiku)
- **Complex reasoning** ("Diagnose this engine failure from sensor data") → large, expensive model (GPT-4o, Claude Opus)
- **Uncertain cases** → start with the small model, check confidence, escalate if needed (cascading)

The routing decision costs almost nothing compared to calling the wrong model. A single GPT-4o call that could have been handled by GPT-4o-mini wastes ~20× the cost. Route 60% of traffic to the cheap tier and you cut your bill in half — with the same user experience.

**Cascading** adds a safety net: instead of making a binary routing decision upfront, you let the cheap model try first. If its answer is confident (high log-probability, matches expected format, passes validation), you ship it. If not, you escalate to the expensive model. This "try cheap first" approach captures the easy wins without risking quality on hard queries.

---

## 🔵 2.6.3 「 How It Works — Step by Step 」

**Model Routing Architecture:**

```
                         ┌──────────────────────┐
                         │   Incoming Request    │
                         │  (query + metadata)   │
                         └──────────┬─────────────┘
                                    │
                                    ▼
                    ┌───────────────────────────────┐
                    │       Router / Classifier      │
                    │                               │
                    │  Features:                    │
                    │  • Query complexity score     │
                    │  • Token count estimate       │
                    │  • Intent / task type         │
                    │  • Latency SLA requirement    │
                    │  • Cost budget remaining      │
                    └──────┬──────┬──────┬──────────┘
                           │      │      │
                  ┌────────┘      │      └────────┐
                  ▼               ▼               ▼
          ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐
          │   Tier 1     │ │   Tier 2     │ │   Tier 3     │
          │  (Cheap)     │ │  (Mid)       │ │  (Premium)   │
          │              │ │              │ │              │
          │ GPT-4o-mini  │ │ GPT-4o      │ │ Claude Opus  │
          │ Claude Haiku │ │ Claude      │ │ GPT-4 Turbo  │
          │              │ │ Sonnet      │ │ o1-preview   │
          │ $0.15/M in   │ │ $2.50/M in  │ │ $15/M in     │
          │ $0.60/M out  │ │ $10/M out   │ │ $75/M out    │
          └──────┬───────┘ └──────┬───────┘ └──────┬───────┘
                 │                │                │
                 └────────┬───────┘────────┬───────┘
                          ▼                ▼
                 ┌──────────────────────────────┐
                 │    Quality Gate / Validator   │
                 │                              │
                 │  • Confidence check          │
                 │  • Format validation         │
                 │  • Length / coherence         │
                 └──────────┬───────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
                 ┌──────────────────────┐
                 │    Final Response    │
                 └──────────────────────┘
```

**Cascading Flow:**

```
  ┌─────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────┐
  │ Request     │────▶│  Tier 1      │────▶│ Quality  │
  │             │     │  (Cheap)     │     │  Gate    │
  └─────────────┘     └──────────────┘     └────┬─────┘
                                                 │
                                    ┌────────────┴────────────┐
                                    │                         │
                              confidence ≥ θ            confidence < θ
                                    │                         │
                                    ▼                         ▼
                           ┌──────────────┐          ┌──────────────┐
                           │ Return as-is │          │  Tier 2      │
                           │  (save $$)   │          │  (Escalate)  │
                           └──────────────┘          └──────┬───────┘
                                                            │
                                                            ▼
                                                     ┌──────────┐
                                                     │ Quality  │
                                                     │  Gate    │
                                                     └────┬─────┘
                                                          │
                                             ┌────────────┴────────────┐
                                             │                         │
                                       confidence ≥ θ            confidence < θ
                                             │                         │
                                             ▼                         ▼
                                    ┌──────────────┐          ┌──────────────┐
                                    │ Return       │          │  Tier 3      │
                                    │              │          │  (Premium)   │
                                    └──────────────┘          └──────────────┘
```

**Step-by-step execution:**

1. **Feature extraction** — Analyze incoming query: token count, detected intent, complexity heuristics (nested questions, domain specificity, reasoning depth), user SLA tier
2. **Route decision** — Router (rule-based, ML classifier, or LLM-as-judge) picks a model tier
3. **Model invocation** — Call the selected model endpoint
4. **Quality gate** — Validate response: confidence scores, format compliance, length, coherence
5. **Cascade check** — If quality gate fails and cheaper tier was used, escalate to next tier
6. **Response delivery** — Return validated response with routing metadata for observability
7. **Feedback loop** — Log routing decisions + outcomes to train/improve the router over time

---

## 🟢 2.6.4 「 Example 1: Rule-Based Model Router 」

<details>
<summary>📝 Click to expand full production code</summary>

```python
"""
Rule-based model router using query features.

Routes requests to appropriate model tiers based on
complexity scoring, token estimates, and task classification.
"""

import re
import tiktoken
from enum import Enum
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional, Dict, Any, List, Tuple


class ModelTier(Enum):
    """Model tiers ordered by cost/capability."""
    CHEAP = "cheap"
    MID = "mid"
    PREMIUM = "premium"


@dataclass
class ModelConfig:
    """Configuration for a model endpoint."""
    name: str
    tier: ModelTier
    provider: str
    cost_per_1k_input: float
    cost_per_1k_output: float
    max_context: int
    avg_latency_ms: float
    strengths: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)


@dataclass
class RoutingDecision:
    """Result of a routing decision with reasoning."""
    model: ModelConfig
    tier: ModelTier
    reason: str
    complexity_score: float
    estimated_tokens: int
    estimated_cost: float
    fallback_model: Optional[ModelConfig] = None


# ── Model Registry ─────────────────────────────────────────────

MODELS: Dict[ModelTier, ModelConfig] = {
    ModelTier.CHEAP: ModelConfig(
        name="gpt-4o-mini",
        tier=ModelTier.CHEAP,
        provider="azure_openai",
        cost_per_1k_input=0.000150,
        cost_per_1k_output=0.000600,
        max_context=128_000,
        avg_latency_ms=800,
        strengths=["greetings", "simple_qa", "classification",
                    "extraction", "summarization_short"],
    ),
    ModelTier.MID: ModelConfig(
        name="gpt-4o",
        tier=ModelTier.MID,
        provider="azure_openai",
        cost_per_1k_input=0.002500,
        cost_per_1k_output=0.010000,
        max_context=128_000,
        avg_latency_ms=2_000,
        strengths=["analysis", "multi_step_reasoning", "code_generation",
                    "summarization_long", "comparison"],
    ),
    ModelTier.PREMIUM: ModelConfig(
        name="o1-preview",
        tier=ModelTier.PREMIUM,
        provider="azure_openai",
        cost_per_1k_input=0.015000,
        cost_per_1k_output=0.060000,
        max_context=128_000,
        avg_latency_ms=8_000,
        strengths=["complex_reasoning", "math", "planning",
                    "multi_constraint", "safety_critical"],
    ),
}


# ── Complexity Scoring ─────────────────────────────────────────

COMPLEXITY_SIGNALS = {
    "multi_step": (
        r"\b(first|then|next|after that|finally|step \d|compare|contrast)\b",
        0.15,
    ),
    "technical_depth": (
        r"\b(algorithm|architecture|optimize|diagnose|root cause|failure mode)\b",
        0.20,
    ),
    "reasoning_required": (
        r"\b(why|explain|analyze|evaluate|pros and cons|trade.?off)\b",
        0.15,
    ),
    "code_generation": (
        r"\b(write|implement|code|function|class|refactor|debug)\b",
        0.20,
    ),
    "long_context": (
        r"\b(document|report|summarize the following|given this context)\b",
        0.10,
    ),
    "safety_critical": (
        r"\b(safety|compliance|legal|medical|financial advice)\b",
        0.25,
    ),
}

SIMPLE_PATTERNS = [
    r"^(hi|hello|hey|good morning|good evening|thanks|thank you)\b",
    r"^what (is|are) (the |a |an )?\w+\??$",
    r"^(yes|no|ok|okay|sure|got it)\b",
]


class RuleBasedRouter:
    """
    Routes requests to model tiers using rule-based complexity scoring.

    Scoring:
      0.0 - 0.25 → CHEAP   (simple greetings, one-line answers)
      0.25 - 0.60 → MID     (moderate reasoning, standard tasks)
      0.60 - 1.0  → PREMIUM (complex reasoning, safety-critical)
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        models: Optional[Dict[ModelTier, ModelConfig]] = None,
        cheap_threshold: float = 0.25,
        premium_threshold: float = 0.60,
        max_cheap_tokens: int = 2_000,
    ):
        self.models = models or MODELS
        self.cheap_threshold = cheap_threshold
        self.premium_threshold = premium_threshold
        self.max_cheap_tokens = max_cheap_tokens
        self._encoder = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4o")

    def route(
        self,
        query: str,
        context: str = "",
        task_type: Optional[str] = None,
        latency_budget_ms: Optional[float] = None,
    ) -> RoutingDecision:
        """
        Route a request to the optimal model tier.

        Args:
            query: User query text
            context: Additional context (documents, history)
            task_type: Explicit task classification (overrides heuristic)
            latency_budget_ms: Maximum acceptable latency

        Returns:
            RoutingDecision with selected model and reasoning
        """
        full_text = f"{query} {context}".strip()
        estimated_tokens = len(self._encoder.encode(full_text))

        complexity = self._score_complexity(query, task_type)

        if latency_budget_ms and latency_budget_ms < 1_500:
            if complexity < self.premium_threshold:
                complexity = min(complexity, self.cheap_threshold - 0.01)

        if estimated_tokens > self.max_cheap_tokens:
            complexity = max(complexity, self.cheap_threshold + 0.01)

        tier = self._complexity_to_tier(complexity)

        model = self.models[tier]

        fallback = None
        if tier != ModelTier.PREMIUM:
            next_tier = (
                ModelTier.MID if tier == ModelTier.CHEAP
                else ModelTier.PREMIUM
            )
            fallback = self.models[next_tier]

        est_output = estimated_tokens * 1.5
        cost = (
            (estimated_tokens / 1000) * model.cost_per_1k_input
            + (est_output / 1000) * model.cost_per_1k_output
        )

        reason = self._build_reason(complexity, tier, estimated_tokens, latency_budget_ms)

        return RoutingDecision(
            model=model,
            tier=tier,
            reason=reason,
            complexity_score=complexity,
            estimated_tokens=estimated_tokens,
            estimated_cost=cost,
            fallback_model=fallback,
        )

    def _score_complexity(
        self, query: str, task_type: Optional[str]
    ) -> float:
        """Score query complexity from 0.0 (trivial) to 1.0 (very complex)."""
        lower = query.lower().strip()

        for pattern in SIMPLE_PATTERNS:
            if re.match(pattern, lower):
                return 0.05

        score = 0.0
        matched_signals = []

        for signal_name, (pattern, weight) in COMPLEXITY_SIGNALS.items():
            if re.search(pattern, lower, re.IGNORECASE):
                score += weight
                matched_signals.append(signal_name)

        word_count = len(lower.split())
        if word_count > 50:
            score += 0.10
        if word_count > 150:
            score += 0.10

        question_marks = lower.count("?")
        if question_marks > 2:
            score += 0.10

        if task_type:
            type_scores = {
                "greeting": 0.05,
                "simple_qa": 0.15,
                "classification": 0.15,
                "extraction": 0.20,
                "summarization": 0.35,
                "analysis": 0.50,
                "code_generation": 0.55,
                "complex_reasoning": 0.75,
                "safety_critical": 0.85,
            }
            if task_type in type_scores:
                score = max(score, type_scores[task_type])

        return min(score, 1.0)

    def _complexity_to_tier(self, score: float) -> ModelTier:
        """Map complexity score to model tier."""
        if score < self.cheap_threshold:
            return ModelTier.CHEAP
        elif score < self.premium_threshold:
            return ModelTier.MID
        else:
            return ModelTier.PREMIUM

    def _build_reason(
        self,
        complexity: float,
        tier: ModelTier,
        tokens: int,
        latency_budget: Optional[float],
    ) -> str:
        """Build human-readable routing reason."""
        parts = [f"complexity={complexity:.2f} → {tier.value}"]
        parts.append(f"est_tokens={tokens}")
        if latency_budget:
            parts.append(f"latency_budget={latency_budget}ms")
        return ", ".join(parts)


# ── Usage ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def demo_rule_based_routing():
    """Demonstrate rule-based routing across query types."""
    router = RuleBasedRouter()

    queries = [
        ("Hello!", "", None),
        ("What is a work order?", "", None),
        (
            "Analyze the maintenance history for asset A-4521 and "
            "compare failure rates across Q1 and Q2",
            "Context: 2000 word maintenance log...",
            "analysis",
        ),
        (
            "Given the vibration sensor data showing 4.2mm/s RMS at "
            "bearing DE, diagnose the root cause and recommend a fix "
            "with safety considerations for a 480V motor",
            "",
            "safety_critical",
        ),
    ]

    for query, context, task_type in queries:
        decision = router.route(query, context, task_type)
        print(f"Query:  {query[:60]}...")
        print(f"  Tier:       {decision.tier.value}")
        print(f"  Model:      {decision.model.name}")
        print(f"  Complexity: {decision.complexity_score:.2f}")
        print(f"  Est. cost:  ${decision.estimated_cost:.6f}")
        print(f"  Reason:     {decision.reason}")
        print(f"  Fallback:   {decision.fallback_model.name if decision.fallback_model else 'None'}")
        print()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    demo_rule_based_routing()
```

</details>

---

## 🟢 2.6.5 「 Example 2: Confidence-Based Cascade Router 」

<details>
<summary>📝 Click to expand full production code</summary>

```python
"""
Confidence-based cascading model router.

Tries the cheapest model first; escalates through tiers
only when the response fails quality/confidence checks.
"""

import time
import logging
import hashlib
from enum import Enum
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional, Dict, Any, List, Callable, Tuple
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


# ── Quality Assessment ──────────────────────────────────────────

@dataclass
class QualityResult:
    """Result of a quality assessment on a model response."""
    passed: bool
    confidence: float          # 0.0 - 1.0
    reason: str
    metrics: Dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)


class QualityGate(ABC):
    """Abstract quality gate for response validation."""

    @abstractmethod
    def assess(self, query: str, response: str, **kwargs) -> QualityResult:
        """Assess whether a response meets quality standards."""
        ...


class HeuristicQualityGate(QualityGate):
    """
    Rule-based quality gate using heuristic signals.

    Checks:
    - Response length relative to query complexity
    - Presence of hedge phrases indicating low confidence
    - Format compliance (if expected_format specified)
    - Coherence: response addresses the query topic
    """

    HEDGE_PHRASES = [
        "i'm not sure", "i don't know", "it's unclear",
        "i cannot determine", "it depends", "more context needed",
        "i'm unable to", "insufficient information",
    ]

    REFUSAL_PHRASES = [
        "as an ai", "i cannot help with", "i'm not able to",
        "please consult a professional",
    ]

    def __init__(
        self,
        min_response_length: int = 20,
        max_hedge_ratio: float = 0.3,
        confidence_threshold: float = 0.7,
    ):
        self.min_response_length = min_response_length
        self.max_hedge_ratio = max_hedge_ratio
        self.confidence_threshold = confidence_threshold

    def assess(self, query: str, response: str, **kwargs) -> QualityResult:
        """Assess response quality using heuristic rules."""
        if not response or not response.strip():
            return QualityResult(
                passed=False, confidence=0.0,
                reason="empty_response"
            )

        confidence = 1.0
        reasons = []
        metrics = {}

        # Length check
        resp_len = len(response.strip())
        metrics["response_length"] = resp_len
        if resp_len < self.min_response_length:
            confidence -= 0.4
            reasons.append(f"too_short({resp_len}<{self.min_response_length})")

        # Hedge phrase detection
        resp_lower = response.lower()
        hedge_count = sum(
            1 for phrase in self.HEDGE_PHRASES
            if phrase in resp_lower
        )
        hedge_ratio = hedge_count / max(len(response.split()), 1)
        metrics["hedge_count"] = hedge_count
        metrics["hedge_ratio"] = hedge_ratio
        if hedge_ratio > self.max_hedge_ratio:
            confidence -= 0.3
            reasons.append(f"high_hedging({hedge_ratio:.2f})")

        # Refusal detection
        refusal_count = sum(
            1 for phrase in self.REFUSAL_PHRASES
            if phrase in resp_lower
        )
        metrics["refusal_count"] = refusal_count
        if refusal_count > 0:
            confidence -= 0.5
            reasons.append(f"refusal_detected({refusal_count})")

        # Topic overlap (basic keyword overlap)
        query_words = set(query.lower().split()) - {
            "the", "a", "an", "is", "are", "what", "how", "why",
            "can", "do", "does", "for", "to", "in", "on", "of",
        }
        resp_words = set(resp_lower.split())
        if query_words:
            overlap = len(query_words & resp_words) / len(query_words)
            metrics["topic_overlap"] = overlap
            if overlap < 0.2:
                confidence -= 0.2
                reasons.append(f"low_topic_overlap({overlap:.2f})")

        confidence = max(0.0, min(1.0, confidence))
        passed = confidence >= self.confidence_threshold

        return QualityResult(
            passed=passed,
            confidence=confidence,
            reason="; ".join(reasons) if reasons else "passed_all_checks",
            metrics=metrics,
        )


# ── Cascade Router ──────────────────────────────────────────────

@dataclass
class CascadeResult:
    """Result from cascade routing with full audit trail."""
    response: str
    model_used: str
    tier_used: int
    total_tiers_tried: int
    total_latency_ms: float
    total_cost: float
    quality_scores: List[Dict[str, Any]]
    escalation_reasons: List[str]


class CascadeRouter:
    """
    Cascading model router: try cheap → escalate on low confidence.

    Tier list is ordered cheapest-first. Each tier gets one attempt.
    If the quality gate passes, return immediately. If not, log the
    reason and escalate to the next tier.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        tiers: List[Dict[str, Any]],
        quality_gate: QualityGate,
        max_escalations: int = 3,
    ):
        """
        Args:
            tiers: Ordered list of model tier configs, each with:
                   {"name": str, "invoke_fn": Callable, "cost_per_1k_in": float,
                    "cost_per_1k_out": float}
            quality_gate: QualityGate instance for response validation
            max_escalations: Maximum number of tier escalations
        """
        self.tiers = tiers
        self.quality_gate = quality_gate
        self.max_escalations = min(max_escalations, len(tiers))

    def route(
        self,
        query: str,
        context: str = "",
        start_tier: int = 0,
        **kwargs,
    ) -> CascadeResult:
        """
        Route with cascading: try each tier until quality gate passes.

        Args:
            query: User query
            context: Additional context
            start_tier: Index of tier to start from (0 = cheapest)

        Returns:
            CascadeResult with response and audit trail
        """
        quality_scores = []
        escalation_reasons = []
        total_latency = 0.0
        total_cost = 0.0
        last_response = ""

        for tier_idx in range(start_tier, self.max_escalations):
            tier = self.tiers[tier_idx]
            tier_name = tier["name"]

            logger.info(f"Cascade: trying tier {tier_idx} ({tier_name})")

            # Invoke model
            t0 = time.monotonic()
            try:
                response = tier["invoke_fn"](query, context)
            except Exception as e:
                logger.error(f"Tier {tier_name} failed: {e}")
                escalation_reasons.append(f"{tier_name}: invoke_error({e})")
                quality_scores.append({
                    "tier": tier_name, "error": str(e),
                    "confidence": 0.0, "passed": False,
                })
                continue
            latency = (time.monotonic() - t0) * 1000
            total_latency += latency

            # Estimate cost
            est_input_tokens = len(query.split()) + len(context.split())
            est_output_tokens = len(response.split())
            tier_cost = (
                (est_input_tokens / 1000) * tier["cost_per_1k_in"]
                + (est_output_tokens / 1000) * tier["cost_per_1k_out"]
            )
            total_cost += tier_cost
            last_response = response

            # Quality gate
            quality = self.quality_gate.assess(query, response, **kwargs)
            quality_scores.append({
                "tier": tier_name,
                "confidence": quality.confidence,
                "passed": quality.passed,
                "reason": quality.reason,
                "latency_ms": latency,
                "cost": tier_cost,
                "metrics": quality.metrics,
            })

            if quality.passed:
                logger.info(
                    f"Cascade: accepted at tier {tier_idx} ({tier_name}), "
                    f"confidence={quality.confidence:.2f}"
                )
                return CascadeResult(
                    response=response,
                    model_used=tier_name,
                    tier_used=tier_idx,
                    total_tiers_tried=tier_idx - start_tier + 1,
                    total_latency_ms=total_latency,
                    total_cost=total_cost,
                    quality_scores=quality_scores,
                    escalation_reasons=escalation_reasons,
                )

            # Escalate
            reason = (
                f"{tier_name}: confidence={quality.confidence:.2f}, "
                f"{quality.reason}"
            )
            escalation_reasons.append(reason)
            logger.warning(f"Cascade: escalating from tier {tier_idx} — {reason}")

        # All tiers exhausted — return last response
        logger.warning("Cascade: all tiers exhausted, returning last response")
        return CascadeResult(
            response=last_response,
            model_used=self.tiers[min(self.max_escalations - 1, len(self.tiers) - 1)]["name"],
            tier_used=self.max_escalations - 1,
            total_tiers_tried=self.max_escalations - start_tier,
            total_latency_ms=total_latency,
            total_cost=total_cost,
            quality_scores=quality_scores,
            escalation_reasons=escalation_reasons,
        )


# ── Usage ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def demo_cascade():
    """Demonstrate cascade routing with mock models."""

    def mock_cheap(query: str, context: str) -> str:
        if len(query.split()) < 5:
            return f"Simple answer to: {query}"
        return "I'm not sure about the details of that complex question."

    def mock_mid(query: str, context: str) -> str:
        return f"Detailed analysis of '{query[:50]}': The key factors are..."

    def mock_premium(query: str, context: str) -> str:
        return (
            f"Comprehensive response to '{query[:50]}': "
            "After careful analysis considering multiple factors, "
            "the root cause is bearing misalignment..."
        )

    tiers = [
        {"name": "gpt-4o-mini", "invoke_fn": mock_cheap,
         "cost_per_1k_in": 0.00015, "cost_per_1k_out": 0.0006},
        {"name": "gpt-4o", "invoke_fn": mock_mid,
         "cost_per_1k_in": 0.0025, "cost_per_1k_out": 0.01},
        {"name": "o1-preview", "invoke_fn": mock_premium,
         "cost_per_1k_in": 0.015, "cost_per_1k_out": 0.06},
    ]

    gate = HeuristicQualityGate(confidence_threshold=0.7)
    cascade = CascadeRouter(tiers=tiers, quality_gate=gate)

    # Simple query — should stop at tier 0
    result = cascade.route("Hello!")
    print(f"Simple: model={result.model_used}, tiers_tried={result.total_tiers_tried}")

    # Complex query — cheap model hedges, should escalate
    result = cascade.route(
        "Diagnose the root cause of bearing failure on asset A-4521 "
        "given vibration readings of 4.2mm/s RMS at DE bearing"
    )
    print(f"Complex: model={result.model_used}, tiers_tried={result.total_tiers_tried}")
    for score in result.quality_scores:
        print(f"  {score['tier']}: confidence={score['confidence']:.2f}, "
              f"passed={score['passed']}, reason={score['reason']}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
    demo_cascade()
```

</details>

---

## 🟢 2.6.6 「 Example 3: LangGraph Model Router with State-Based Routing 」

<details>
<summary>📝 Click to expand full production code</summary>

```python
"""
LangGraph-based model router with state-driven routing decisions.

Integrates model routing directly into a LangGraph StateGraph,
using conditional edges to dispatch to different model nodes.
"""

import time
import logging
from enum import Enum
from typing import Any, Dict, List, Optional, Annotated
from dataclasses import dataclass

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


# ── State Definition ────────────────────────────────────────────

class ModelTier(str, Enum):
    CHEAP = "cheap"
    MID = "mid"
    PREMIUM = "premium"


class RouterState(BaseModel):
    """State for the model routing graph."""
    query: str = ""
    context: str = ""
    complexity_score: float = 0.0
    selected_tier: Optional[str] = None
    response: Optional[str] = None
    confidence: float = 0.0
    model_used: Optional[str] = None
    escalation_count: int = 0
    max_escalations: int = 2
    total_cost: float = 0.0
    routing_log: List[Dict[str, Any]] = Field(default_factory=list)


# ── Node Functions ──────────────────────────────────────────────

def analyze_complexity(state: RouterState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Score query complexity and select initial tier."""
    query = state.query.lower()
    score = 0.0

    word_count = len(query.split())
    if word_count > 50:
        score += 0.2
    if word_count > 100:
        score += 0.1

    complex_keywords = [
        "analyze", "diagnose", "compare", "evaluate",
        "root cause", "trade-off", "optimize", "architecture",
    ]
    for kw in complex_keywords:
        if kw in query:
            score += 0.15

    question_count = query.count("?")
    if question_count > 1:
        score += 0.1 * min(question_count, 3)

    score = min(score, 1.0)

    if score < 0.25:
        tier = ModelTier.CHEAP.value
    elif score < 0.60:
        tier = ModelTier.MID.value
    else:
        tier = ModelTier.PREMIUM.value

    logger.info(f"Complexity: {score:.2f} → tier={tier}")
    return {
        "complexity_score": score,
        "selected_tier": tier,
    }


def invoke_cheap_model(state: RouterState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Invoke the cheap tier model (GPT-4o-mini / Claude Haiku)."""
    t0 = time.monotonic()
    # In production: response = cheap_llm.invoke(messages).content
    query = state.query
    if len(query.split()) < 10:
        response = f"Quick answer: {query}"
        confidence = 0.9
    else:
        response = f"Basic response to: {query[:100]}..."
        confidence = 0.5
    latency = (time.monotonic() - t0) * 1000
    cost = len(query.split()) * 0.00000015 + len(response.split()) * 0.0000006

    return {
        "response": response,
        "confidence": confidence,
        "model_used": "gpt-4o-mini",
        "total_cost": state.total_cost + cost,
        "routing_log": state.routing_log + [{
            "tier": "cheap", "model": "gpt-4o-mini",
            "confidence": confidence, "latency_ms": latency, "cost": cost,
        }],
    }


def invoke_mid_model(state: RouterState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Invoke the mid tier model (GPT-4o / Claude Sonnet)."""
    t0 = time.monotonic()
    query = state.query
    response = f"Detailed analysis for: {query[:100]}. The key insight is..."
    confidence = 0.85
    latency = (time.monotonic() - t0) * 1000
    cost = len(query.split()) * 0.0000025 + len(response.split()) * 0.00001

    return {
        "response": response,
        "confidence": confidence,
        "model_used": "gpt-4o",
        "total_cost": state.total_cost + cost,
        "routing_log": state.routing_log + [{
            "tier": "mid", "model": "gpt-4o",
            "confidence": confidence, "latency_ms": latency, "cost": cost,
        }],
    }


def invoke_premium_model(state: RouterState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Invoke the premium tier model (o1-preview / Claude Opus)."""
    t0 = time.monotonic()
    query = state.query
    response = (
        f"Comprehensive analysis: {query[:100]}. "
        "After systematic evaluation of all factors, "
        "the recommended approach considers safety, cost, and reliability..."
    )
    confidence = 0.95
    latency = (time.monotonic() - t0) * 1000
    cost = len(query.split()) * 0.000015 + len(response.split()) * 0.00006

    return {
        "response": response,
        "confidence": confidence,
        "model_used": "o1-preview",
        "total_cost": state.total_cost + cost,
        "routing_log": state.routing_log + [{
            "tier": "premium", "model": "o1-preview",
            "confidence": confidence, "latency_ms": latency, "cost": cost,
        }],
    }


def quality_check(state: RouterState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Check response quality; decide whether to accept or escalate."""
    confidence = state.confidence
    response = state.response or ""

    if len(response.strip()) < 20:
        confidence = min(confidence, 0.3)

    hedge_phrases = ["i'm not sure", "it depends", "unclear"]
    for phrase in hedge_phrases:
        if phrase in response.lower():
            confidence = min(confidence, 0.4)

    return {"confidence": confidence}


# ── Conditional Edges ───────────────────────────────────────────

def route_by_tier(state: RouterState) -> str:
    """Route to model node based on selected tier."""
    tier = state.selected_tier
    if tier == ModelTier.PREMIUM.value:
        return "invoke_premium"
    elif tier == ModelTier.MID.value:
        return "invoke_mid"
    return "invoke_cheap"


def check_or_escalate(state: RouterState) -> str:
    """Accept response or escalate to next tier."""
    if state.confidence >= 0.7:
        return "accept"

    if state.escalation_count >= state.max_escalations:
        return "accept"

    current_tier = state.selected_tier
    if current_tier == ModelTier.CHEAP.value:
        return "escalate_to_mid"
    elif current_tier == ModelTier.MID.value:
        return "escalate_to_premium"
    return "accept"


def escalate_to_mid(state: RouterState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Escalate routing decision to mid tier."""
    logger.info(f"Escalating from {state.selected_tier} to mid")
    return {
        "selected_tier": ModelTier.MID.value,
        "escalation_count": state.escalation_count + 1,
    }


def escalate_to_premium(state: RouterState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Escalate routing decision to premium tier."""
    logger.info(f"Escalating from {state.selected_tier} to premium")
    return {
        "selected_tier": ModelTier.PREMIUM.value,
        "escalation_count": state.escalation_count + 1,
    }


# ── Graph Construction ──────────────────────────────────────────

def build_routing_graph() -> StateGraph:
    """Build the model routing LangGraph."""
    graph = StateGraph(RouterState)

    # Add nodes
    graph.add_node("analyze", analyze_complexity)
    graph.add_node("invoke_cheap", invoke_cheap_model)
    graph.add_node("invoke_mid", invoke_mid_model)
    graph.add_node("invoke_premium", invoke_premium_model)
    graph.add_node("quality_check", quality_check)
    graph.add_node("escalate_to_mid", escalate_to_mid)
    graph.add_node("escalate_to_premium", escalate_to_premium)

    # Entry
    graph.set_entry_point("analyze")

    # Route from analysis to model tier
    graph.add_conditional_edges(
        "analyze",
        route_by_tier,
        {
            "invoke_cheap": "invoke_cheap",
            "invoke_mid": "invoke_mid",
            "invoke_premium": "invoke_premium",
        },
    )

    # All model invocations → quality check
    for node in ["invoke_cheap", "invoke_mid", "invoke_premium"]:
        graph.add_edge(node, "quality_check")

    # Quality check → accept or escalate
    graph.add_conditional_edges(
        "quality_check",
        check_or_escalate,
        {
            "accept": END,
            "escalate_to_mid": "escalate_to_mid",
            "escalate_to_premium": "escalate_to_premium",
        },
    )

    # Escalation nodes → re-invoke the target tier
    graph.add_edge("escalate_to_mid", "invoke_mid")
    graph.add_edge("escalate_to_premium", "invoke_premium")

    return graph


# ── Usage ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def demo_langgraph_routing():
    """Demonstrate LangGraph-based model routing."""
    graph = build_routing_graph()
    app = graph.compile()

    # Simple query
    result = app.invoke({"query": "Hello!"})
    print(f"Simple: model={result['model_used']}, "
          f"confidence={result['confidence']:.2f}, "
          f"cost=${result['total_cost']:.6f}")

    # Complex query
    result = app.invoke({
        "query": "Analyze the root cause of repeated bearing failures "
                 "on asset A-4521 and compare preventive vs predictive "
                 "maintenance strategies with cost trade-offs"
    })
    print(f"Complex: model={result['model_used']}, "
          f"confidence={result['confidence']:.2f}, "
          f"cost=${result['total_cost']:.6f}")
    for entry in result["routing_log"]:
        print(f"  {entry['tier']}: confidence={entry['confidence']:.2f}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
    demo_langgraph_routing()
```

</details>

---

## 🟢 2.6.7 「 How to Implement (Production Guide) 」

### Step 1: Define Your Model Tiers

```python
TIER_CONFIG = {
    "cheap":   {"model": "gpt-4o-mini",  "cost_in": 0.15,  "cost_out": 0.60,  "max_ctx": 128_000},
    "mid":     {"model": "gpt-4o",       "cost_in": 2.50,  "cost_out": 10.00, "max_ctx": 128_000},
    "premium": {"model": "o1-preview",   "cost_in": 15.00, "cost_out": 60.00, "max_ctx": 128_000},
}
```

### Step 2: Implement Complexity Scoring

Start with rules, graduate to ML when you have routing logs:

| Phase | Router Type | Data Needed | Accuracy |
|-------|------------|-------------|----------|
| **Phase 1** | Regex + heuristics | None | ~70% |
| **Phase 2** | Lightweight classifier (fasttext/logistic) | 1K labeled routes | ~85% |
| **Phase 3** | Fine-tuned small LM | 10K labeled routes | ~92% |
| **Phase 4** | Online learning (bandit) | Continuous feedback | ~95% |

### Step 3: Build Quality Gates

Quality gates must be **faster and cheaper** than the model they guard:

```python
# Rule: quality gate latency < 50ms, cost = $0
# If your gate itself calls an LLM, you've lost the savings
gate_budget = {
    "latency_ms": 50,
    "cost": 0.0,
    "checks": ["length", "hedge_detection", "format_compliance", "topic_overlap"],
}
```

### Step 4: Add Observability

Every routing decision needs metrics:

```python
routing_metrics = {
    "query_hash": "sha256(query)[:8]",
    "complexity_score": 0.42,
    "initial_tier": "cheap",
    "final_tier": "mid",
    "escalations": 1,
    "total_latency_ms": 2_450,
    "total_cost_usd": 0.003,
    "quality_confidence": 0.82,
}
```

### Step 5: Implement Feedback Loop

```python
# After user feedback or downstream quality signal:
# 1. Log the routing decision + outcome
# 2. Periodically retrain the router on (query_features, optimal_tier) pairs
# 3. A/B test router versions before promoting
```

---

## 🔵 2.6.8 「 Comparison Table (Approaches) 」

| Approach | Latency Overhead | Cost Savings | Quality Risk | Complexity | Best For |
|----------|-----------------|-------------|-------------|-----------|---------|
| **Single model (no routing)** | 0ms | 0% | None | Trivial | Prototypes, low volume |
| **Rule-based routing** | <5ms | 30-50% | Low (predictable) | Low | Known task taxonomy |
| **ML classifier routing** | 10-30ms | 40-60% | Medium (training drift) | Medium | High volume, varied queries |
| **LLM-as-judge routing** | 200-500ms | 20-40% | Low | Medium | When accuracy > speed |
| **Confidence cascade** | Varies (multi-call) | 30-55% | Very low | Medium | When quality is paramount |
| **Bandit/RL routing** | <5ms | 50-70% | Low (self-correcting) | High | Large scale, continuous learning |
| **Embedding similarity** | 20-50ms | 35-50% | Medium | Medium | Cluster-based routing |
| **Hybrid (rules + cascade)** | 5ms + cascade | 45-65% | Very low | Medium-high | Production systems |

---

## 🟠 2.6.9 「 When and Where to Use This Pattern 」

### Use When

- **Traffic is bimodal**: Most queries simple, some complex (60/30/10 split common)
- **Cost is a concern**: Monthly LLM spend > $500 and growing
- **Multiple models available**: Access to at least 2 tiers (cheap + capable)
- **Quality tolerance varies**: Not every query needs GPT-4-level reasoning
- **Latency budgets differ**: Some paths need <1s, others can take 5s

### Avoid When

- **All queries are hard**: If 90%+ need the premium model, routing adds overhead for nothing
- **Volume is tiny**: Below ~1K requests/day, the engineering cost exceeds savings
- **Single model mandate**: Compliance or contractual requirement to use one specific model
- **Real-time cascade is unacceptable**: If cascading doubles latency and SLA is tight
- **No quality signal**: Without a way to measure response quality, routing decisions are blind

### Domain Examples

| Domain | Cheap Tier Handles | Premium Tier Handles |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Maintenance copilot** | Greetings, WO briefing lookup, simple FAQ | Root cause diagnosis, multi-asset comparison |
| **Customer support** | Order status, FAQ, password reset | Complaint escalation, refund decisions |
| **Code assistant** | Autocomplete, docstrings, simple functions | Architecture design, debugging complex bugs |
| **Medical triage** | Symptom logging, appointment scheduling | Differential diagnosis (always with human review) |

---

## ✅ 2.6.9b 「 Do's and Don'ts 」

### ✅ Do's

1. **Start with rules, graduate to ML** — Rule-based routing gets you 70% of the savings with 10% of the complexity
2. **Log every routing decision** — You cannot improve what you cannot measure
3. **Set a cost ceiling per request** — Prevent cascading from running through all tiers on adversarial inputs
4. **Test routing on production traffic shadows** — Route both ways, compare quality, before switching
5. **Include a manual override** — Allow forcing a specific tier for debugging or VIP users
6. **Keep quality gates fast** — A quality gate that calls an LLM defeats the purpose
7. **Version your router** — Track which router version made each decision for A/B analysis

### ❌ Don'ts

1. **Don't route by user identity alone** — A "junior" user can ask complex questions
2. **Don't ignore the cascade cost** — Calling cheap + mid + premium costs more than just premium
3. **Don't hardcode thresholds forever** — Query distributions shift; review quarterly
4. **Don't use cascading for streaming** — You cannot un-stream tokens from the cheap model
5. **Don't skip the fallback** — If routing fails (classifier error), default to mid tier, not crash
6. **Don't route safety-critical queries to cheap** — Compliance, medical, legal → always premium
7. **Don't over-optimize early** — Start with 2 tiers; add a third only when data shows the need

---

## ⚪ 2.6.10 「 Pros and Cons 」

### Pros

| Advantage | Details |
|-----------|---------|
| **Cost reduction** | 40-70% savings by routing simple queries to cheap models |
| **Maintained quality** | Premium model still handles complex queries |
| **Latency improvement** | Simple queries answered faster by smaller models |
| **Scalability** | Cheap models handle higher throughput |
| **Graceful degradation** | Cascade provides automatic fallback |
| **Observability** | Routing logs reveal query distribution patterns |
| **Progressive enhancement** | Start with rules, add ML routing incrementally |

### Cons

| Disadvantage | Details |
|--------------|---------|
| **Complexity overhead** | Router is additional infrastructure to maintain |
| **Cascade latency** | Failed tiers add latency before escalation |
| **Quality gate errors** | False positives (accepting bad responses) reduce quality |
| **Router accuracy** | Misrouted queries get wrong model (too cheap or too expensive) |
| **Testing burden** | Must test all routing paths × all model tiers |
| **Cold start** | No routing data means rules only until you have logs |
| **Model API changes** | Model updates can shift quality thresholds |

---

## 🟠 2.6.11 「 The DC-Copilot Connection 」

### Current State: Single Model, No Routing

DC-Copilot currently uses a **single Azure OpenAI model** for all requests regardless of complexity. Examining the source:

**`copilot/copilot_api/services/llm_service.py`** (line 22-42):
```python
class LLMService:
    def __init__(self, llm: Any, logger: Optional[logging.Logger] = None):
        self.llm = llm
        self.logger = logger or logging.getLogger(__name__)
```

Single `llm` instance — no model selection logic. Every request (greetings, complex diagnosis, maintenance history) hits the same model at the same cost.

**`configs/copilot_config.py`** (line 242-257):
```python
required_categories = [
    "database", "storage", "vectordb",
    "openai", "llm", "opensearch", "text_processing",
]
```

One `llm` category and one `openai` category — no multi-model configuration. The config validates exactly one set of LLM credentials.

**The routing opportunity is already partially built.** DC-Copilot's `ClassifyIntentNode` already classifies every query into one of 10 intents:

```python
# From copilot/copilot_api/nodes/intent_types.py
class IntentType:
    GREETINGS = "greetings"           # → CHEAP tier candidate
    META_ANSWER = "meta_answer"       # → CHEAP tier candidate
    GENERAL = "general"               # → MID tier
    CONTEXTUAL = "contextual"         # → MID tier
    DIAGNOSIS = "diagnosis"           # → PREMIUM tier candidate
    FIX = "fix"                       # → PREMIUM tier candidate
    BOTH_DIAGNOSTIC_FIX = "..."       # → PREMIUM tier candidate
    WORKORDER_BRIEFING = "..."        # → CHEAP tier (template lookup)
    MAINTENANCE_HISTORY = "..."       # → CHEAP tier (data retrieval)
    COMBINE_WORKORDER_ASSET_HISTORY = "..."  # → MID tier
```

This intent classification is a **free routing signal** — it already runs before the LLM call and directly maps to complexity tiers.

### Proposed Enhancement: Intent-Based Model Routing

```
  ┌──────────────┐     ┌────────────────────┐
  │  Classify     │────▶│  IntentModelRouter  │
  │  Intent       │     │  (new node)         │
  └──────────────┘     └────┬────┬────┬──────┘
                             │    │    │
               ┌─────────────┘    │    └──────────────┐
               ▼                  ▼                   ▼
     ┌──────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
     │ GPT-4o-mini  │   │ GPT-4o       │    │ o1-preview   │
     │ (greetings,  │   │ (general,    │    │ (diagnosis,  │
     │  meta, WO    │   │  contextual, │    │  fix, both   │
     │  briefing,   │   │  combine)    │    │  diag+fix)   │
     │  maintenance)│   │              │    │              │
     │              │   │              │    │              │
     │  ~60% traffic│   │ ~30% traffic │    │ ~10% traffic │
     └──────────────┘   └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘
```

**Implementation in LLMService:**

```python
class RoutingLLMService(LLMService):
    """LLMService with intent-based model routing."""

    INTENT_TIER_MAP = {
        "greetings": "cheap",
        "meta_answer": "cheap",
        "workorder_briefing": "cheap",
        "maintenance_history": "cheap",
        "general": "mid",
        "contextual": "mid",
        "combine_workorder_asset_history": "mid",
        "diagnosis": "premium",
        "fix": "premium",
        "both_diagnostic_fix": "premium",
    }

    def __init__(self, llm_pool: Dict[str, Any], logger=None):
        self.llm_pool = llm_pool  # {"cheap": mini_llm, "mid": gpt4o, "premium": o1}
        self.logger = logger or logging.getLogger(__name__)

    def select_llm(self, intent: str) -> Any:
        tier = self.INTENT_TIER_MAP.get(intent, "mid")
        return self.llm_pool[tier]
```

**Config change needed in `copilot_config.py`:**

```python
required_categories = [
    "database", "storage", "vectordb",
    "openai", "openai_mini", "openai_premium",  # ← multi-model
    "llm", "opensearch", "text_processing",
]
```

**Estimated savings** (based on DC-Copilot's intent distribution):

| Intent | % Traffic | Current Cost | With Routing | Savings |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|---------|
| Greetings + Meta | ~15% | $2.50/M in | $0.15/M in | 94% |
| WO Briefing + History | ~45% | $2.50/M in | $0.15/M in | 94% |
| General + Contextual | ~25% | $2.50/M in | $2.50/M in | 0% |
| Diagnosis + Fix | ~15% | $2.50/M in | $15.00/M in | -500% |
| **Weighted average** | **100%** | **$2.50/M** | **~$1.20/M** | **~52%** |

The `stream()` method constraint (line 155-229) means cascading is not viable for streaming responses — once tokens are yielded, you cannot retry with a different model. Intent-based routing (decide model **before** calling) is the right pattern for DC-Copilot's architecture.

---

## 🔴 2.6.12 「 Anti-Patterns Gallery 」

### Anti-Pattern 1: LLM-as-Router

```python
# ❌ Using a large LLM to decide which model to use
def route_with_llm(query: str) -> str:
    response = gpt4.invoke(
        f"Classify this query as simple/medium/complex: {query}"
    )
    tier = parse_tier(response)
    return tier  # Cost: spent GPT-4 tokens just to decide
```

**Why it fails:** The routing call costs as much as the cheap model would cost for the entire query. Net savings = negative for simple queries.

**Fix:** Use heuristics, fasttext, or a fine-tuned tiny classifier (<1ms, $0).

### Anti-Pattern 2: Cascade for Streaming

```python
# ❌ Cascading with streaming responses
async def cascade_stream(query: str):
    async for token in cheap_model.stream(query):
        yield token  # Already sent to client!
    # Quality check fails...
    # Can't unsend tokens, can't retry
```

**Why it fails:** Streaming is irreversible. Once you yield tokens, the client has them. Cascading requires the ability to discard and retry.

**Fix:** For streaming, use upfront routing (decide model before calling). Reserve cascading for non-streaming endpoints.

### Anti-Pattern 3: Routing Without Logging

```python
# ❌ Black-box routing with no audit trail
def route(query):
    if is_simple(query):
        return call_cheap(query)
    return call_expensive(query)
    # No logging → can't measure accuracy, can't improve
```

**Fix:** Log every routing decision with features, selected tier, quality score, and cost.

### Anti-Pattern 4: Routing on User Tier, Not Query

```python
# ❌ Giving free users cheap model regardless of query
def route_by_user(query, user):
    if user.plan == "free":
        return call_cheap(query)  # "Diagnose engine failure" → Haiku
    return call_expensive(query)
```

**Why it fails:** User tier determines who pays, not what model is needed. A free user asking a complex question still deserves the right model — or an honest "upgrade for advanced analysis."

---

## ⚪ 2.6.13 「 Performance Benchmark Table 」

| Metric | No Routing | Rule-Based | ML Classifier | Cascade (2-tier) | Cascade (3-tier) |
|--------|-----------|-----------|--------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Avg latency (simple)** | 2,000ms | 800ms | 820ms | 850ms | 850ms |
| **Avg latency (complex)** | 2,000ms | 8,000ms | 8,020ms | 2,900ms* | 4,800ms* |
| **P99 latency** | 5,000ms | 12,000ms | 12,050ms | 14,000ms | 18,000ms |
| **Cost per 1K requests** | $2.50 | $1.10 | $0.95 | $1.20 | $1.35 |
| **Quality (BLEU/human)** | Baseline | -2% | -1% | +0.5% | +1% |
| **Router overhead** | 0ms | <5ms | 15ms | 0ms | 0ms |
| **Accuracy (correct tier)** | N/A | 72% | 88% | N/A (auto) | N/A (auto) |
| **Implementation time** | 0 hours | 4 hours | 16 hours | 8 hours | 12 hours |
| **Maintenance burden** | None | Low | Medium | Medium | High |

*Cascade latency for complex queries reflects escalation: cheap attempt (~800ms) + quality check (~50ms) + mid/premium attempt.

**Key finding:** Rule-based routing delivers 56% cost savings with <5ms overhead and 4 hours of implementation. ML classifier adds ~15% more savings but requires training data and maintenance.

## 🟢 2.6.15 「 Migration Path 」

### Step 0 → Baseline: Single Model

```
Current state:
  All requests → GPT-4o → Response
  Cost: $2.50/1K input, $10.00/1K output
  No routing logic, no quality measurement
```

### Step 1 → Shadow Routing (Week 1-2)

```python
# Log what routing WOULD do without changing behavior
class ShadowRouter:
    def __init__(self, router, actual_model):
        self.router = router
        self.actual_model = actual_model

    def invoke(self, query, **kwargs):
        # Always use actual model
        response = self.actual_model.invoke(query, **kwargs)
        # Log what routing would have done
        decision = self.router.route(query)
        logger.info(f"shadow_route: query_hash={hash(query)}, "
                    f"would_route_to={decision.tier}, actual=mid")
        return response
```

### Step 2 → Rule-Based Routing for Simple Intents (Week 3-4)

```python
# Route ONLY known-simple intents; everything else → existing model
SAFE_CHEAP_INTENTS = {"greetings", "meta_answer"}

def route(intent: str, llm_pool: dict) -> Any:
    if intent in SAFE_CHEAP_INTENTS:
        return llm_pool["cheap"]
    return llm_pool["mid"]  # Current model
```

### Step 3 → Full Intent-Based Routing (Week 5-8)

```python
# Expand routing to all intents based on shadow routing data
INTENT_TIER_MAP = {
    "greetings": "cheap",
    "meta_answer": "cheap",
    "workorder_briefing": "cheap",
    "maintenance_history": "cheap",
    "general": "mid",
    "contextual": "mid",
    "combine_workorder_asset_history": "mid",
    "diagnosis": "premium",
    "fix": "premium",
    "both_diagnostic_fix": "premium",
}
```

### Step 4 → Add Cascade for Non-Streaming (Week 9-12)

```python
# For /summary endpoint (non-streaming): add cascade
# For /chat endpoint (streaming): keep upfront routing
class HybridRouter:
    def route_streaming(self, intent):
        return INTENT_TIER_MAP.get(intent, "mid")  # Upfront only

    def route_non_streaming(self, intent, query):
        tier = INTENT_TIER_MAP.get(intent, "mid")
        # Start at mapped tier, cascade up if quality fails
        return CascadeRouter(start_tier=tier)
```

---

## 💡 2.6.16 「 Interview / Viva Q&A 」

**Q1: How do you decide between upfront routing and cascading?**

**A:** It depends on whether the response is reversible. For **streaming endpoints** (SSE, WebSockets), you must use upfront routing because once tokens are yielded to the client, you cannot retract them. For **non-streaming endpoints** (batch jobs, summarization APIs), cascading is viable because you can discard a response and retry with a better model. The decision matrix:

| Endpoint Type | Response Reversible? | Strategy |
|--------------|---------------------|----------|
| Streaming SSE | No | Upfront routing |
| REST + buffered | Yes | Cascade or upfront |
| Batch / async | Yes | Cascade preferred |
| Real-time + SLA | Depends on SLA | Upfront if SLA tight, cascade if flexible |

**Q2: What's the cheapest way to build a model router?**

**A:** A lookup table mapping task_type/intent → model tier. Zero ML, zero latency overhead, gets ~70% of optimal savings. The full progression: (1) lookup table ($0, 4 hours), (2) regex complexity scorer ($0, 8 hours), (3) fasttext classifier ($0, 16 hours + labeling), (4) fine-tuned DistilBERT ($50 training cost, 40 hours), (5) bandit learner ($0, 80+ hours). Most teams should stop at step 2 or 3.

**Q3: How do you measure routing accuracy?**

**A:** There's no single ground truth, but three proxy metrics work well: (1) **Cost regret** — how much more did the actual route cost vs. the cheapest tier that would have produced an acceptable response? This requires sampling and evaluating at all tiers. (2) **Quality delta** — compare response quality (human eval or auto-eval) between the routed tier and the premium tier. If delta < threshold, routing was correct. (3) **Escalation rate** — in cascade routing, track what percentage of requests escalate. High escalation (~40%+) suggests the cheap tier threshold is too aggressive.

**Q4: How do you handle model API changes or deprecations?**

**A:** Treat models as interchangeable behind an interface. Your routing table maps to tier names ("cheap", "mid", "premium"), not model names. When a model is deprecated: (1) update the tier→model mapping in config, (2) re-run quality benchmarks on a held-out test set, (3) adjust thresholds if the new model's quality profile differs. Never hardcode model names in routing logic.

**Q5: Can you route within the same model family (e.g., GPT-4o with different temperatures)?**

**A:** Yes — this is "parameter routing" and can be more cost-effective than model routing because you avoid the overhead of multiple model deployments. Route by: (1) **temperature** — 0.0 for factual lookups, 0.7 for creative tasks; (2) **max_tokens** — cap at 100 for simple answers, 2000 for analysis; (3) **system prompt length** — minimal for greetings, full context for diagnosis. Parameter routing can cut output token costs 30-50% even with a single model.

---

## ✅ 2.6.17 「 Production Readiness Checklist 」

- [ ] Model tiers defined with cost, latency, and capability profiles
- [ ] Router (rule-based or ML) classifies incoming requests
- [ ] Quality gate validates responses without calling additional LLMs
- [ ] Fallback tier configured (default to mid if router fails)
- [ ] Streaming endpoints use upfront routing only (no cascade)
- [ ] Non-streaming endpoints support cascade with configurable depth
- [ ] Every routing decision logged with: query_hash, features, tier, cost, quality_score
- [ ] Cost ceiling per request prevents runaway cascade spending
- [ ] Shadow routing mode available for safe rollout of routing changes
- [ ] A/B test framework compares routing versions on live traffic
- [ ] Alerts on routing anomalies: escalation rate spike, cost spike, quality drop
- [ ] Model tier configuration externalised (config file, not hardcoded)

---

## 🟡 2.6.18 「 Troubleshooting Guide 」

| Symptom | Check | Fix |
|---------|-------|-----|
| All requests route to premium tier | Complexity thresholds too low | Raise cheap_threshold from 0.15 to 0.25 |
| All requests route to cheap tier | Complexity scoring misses signals | Add domain-specific keywords to scorer |
| Cascade always escalates | Quality gate threshold too strict | Lower confidence threshold from 0.9 to 0.7 |
| Cascade never escalates | Quality gate too lenient | Raise threshold; add hedge-phrase detection |
| Cost increased after adding routing | Cascade overhead > savings | Limit max escalations; increase upfront accuracy |
| Latency spikes on complex queries | Cascade tries all tiers | Skip cheap tier for known-complex intents |
| Quality regression on simple queries | Cheap model can't handle edge cases | Add format validation in quality gate |
| Router drift after model update | Model quality profile changed | Re-benchmark and adjust tier thresholds |
| Shadow router disagrees with live router | Config divergence | Sync shadow and live configs in CI/CD |
| Router crashes on empty query | Missing input validation | Add null/empty checks before scoring |

---

| Output cost | $30.00/day | $11.80/day | $13.80/day | 61% | 54% |

| **Enables** | Ch 2.10: Cost Dashboard | Routing metadata (tier, cost, escalations) powers cost attribution dashboards |
| **Parallels** | Ch 1.6: Error Recovery | Cascade escalation mirrors retry-with-fallback; quality gates mirror response validation |
| **Parallels** | Ch 1.10: LangGraph vs Alternatives | Framework choice affects routing integration (LangGraph conditional edges vs custom dispatch) |

The savings compound: combine routing with prompt compression (Ch 2.1) and semantic caching (Ch 2.2) for total cost reduction of 70-85%.
